suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Someone who has the four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and ethical conduct—is a stream-enterer, destined for awakening.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about the rebirths of several disciples. The Buddha confirms those details, and proposes a way for people to know for themselves by reflecting on the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta explains to Ānanda that someone who has the four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and ethical conduct—is a stream-enterer, destined for awakening.

suttas.title: 6. A Wise Person
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple with the four factors of stream-entry is a stream-enterer. With verses.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: The Buddha contrasts a foolish brahmanical ritual called “going upwards” with the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: You should encourage your friends in the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: The Buddha asks Sāriputta about the four factors for stream-entry: association with good people, hearing the teaching, proper attention, and right practice. He also defines the “stream” and the “stream-enterer”.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: The Buddha visits the gods of the Thirty-Three and encourages them in the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: The Buddha teaches the four factors of stream-entry to Nandaka, the minister of the Licchavis. When his servant tells him it is time for his ritual bath, he says that the inner bathing of the Dhamma is enough.

suttas.title: 5. Householders
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple who is a layperson has eliminated the fear that comes from breaking precepts, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and understands dependent origination.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: Mahānāma the Sakyan expresses his fear that if he dies unmindful he may go to a bad rebirth. The Buddha tells him not to fear, as he will definitely go to a good place, since he has the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 3. Householders
suttas.blurb: Questioned by Jīvaka Komārabhacca, the Buddha explains what makes someone a Buddhist lay follower, a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower practicing for the welfare of all.

suttas.title: 4. Overflowing Merit
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to Mahānāma what is a lay follower, what is a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower accomplished in faith, generosity, and wisdom.

suttas.title: 6. A Wise Person
suttas.blurb: The lay follower Dhammadinna leads a group of 500 to visit the Buddha. He encourages them to study deep discourses, but they protest that they don’t have enough time. So the Buddha encourages them in the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 3. Abodes of Sentient Beings
suttas.blurb: A householder who has eliminated the perils that come with breaking the five precepts, and possesses the four factors of stream-entry is freed from lower rebirths.

suttas.title: 6. A Wise Person
suttas.blurb: When a monk comes from Sāvatthī to Kapilavatthu, the Sakyans visit him and ask as to the health of the Buddha and the Saṅgha. Then they ask what the monk has learned. He replies that the number of perfected ones is few, and those on lower levels of attainment are greater in number.

suttas.title: 3. Householders
suttas.blurb: Questioned by his relative Mahānāma, the Buddha explains what makes someone a Buddhist lay follower, a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower practicing for the welfare of all.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Even a universal monarch may have a bad rebirth, but someone who has the four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha, and ethical conduct—is freed from such destinies.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about the rebirths of several disciples. The Buddha confirms those details, and proposes a way for people to know for themselves by reflecting on the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: Mahānāma the Sakyan expresses his fear that if he dies unmindful he may go to a bad rebirth. The Buddha tells him not to fear, as he will definitely go to a good place, since he has long practiced the Dhamma.

suttas.title: 3. Abodes of Sentient Beings
suttas.blurb: A householder who has eliminated the perils that come with breaking the five precepts, and possesses the four factors of stream-entry is freed from lower rebirths.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about the rebirths of several disciples. The Buddha confirms those details, and proposes a way for people to know for themselves by reflecting on the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: Mahānāma and Godhā the Sakyans discuss whether a stream-enterer has three or four factors. They go to the Buddha to settle it, and Mahānāma testifies to his faith.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: The lay follower Dīghāvu is ill and he asks for the Buddha to visit. Dīghāvu confirms that he possesses the four factors of stream-entry and in addition six contemplations leading to insight. Yet he is still worried that his father will miss him when he dies.

suttas.title: 5. Householders
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple who is a layperson has eliminated the fear that comes from breaking precepts, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and understands dependent origination.

suttas.title: 18. A Lay Follower
suttas.blurb: Qualities possessing which a lay person may claim to be a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 6. A Wise Person
suttas.blurb: Mahānāma asks the Buddha how a lay follower should teach a wise lay follower who is ill. He should first be encouraged in the four factors of stream-entry, and then urged to let go of attachments, until he realizes full freedom.

suttas.title: 10. With Upāli
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple has eliminated five perils, realized the four factors of stream-entry, and seen the noble process.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple who is a layperson has eliminated the fear that comes from breaking precepts, possesses the four factors of stream-entry, and understands dependent origination.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: When Sarakāni the Sakyan died the Buddha declared him a stream-enterer. But others complained, since he was known as a drinker. However, the Buddha confirmed to Mahānāma that Sarakāni had undertaken the precepts on his deathbed.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: After the rainy season, the chamberlains Isidatta and Purāṇa hear that the Buddha is approaching and go to see him. They describe their joy at knowing when the Buddha is approaching, and speak of their special duties guarding the royal harem. The Buddha says they have four factors of stream-entry—experiential faith in the Buddha, the teaching, and the Saṅgha; and here generosity rather than ethics as the final factor.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: When Sarakāni the Sakyan died the Buddha declared him a stream-enterer. But others complained, since he was known as a drinker. However, the Buddha confirmed to Mahānāma that Sarakāni had undertaken the precepts on his deathbed.

suttas.title: 1. At Bamboo Gate
suttas.blurb: The brahmin householders of Bamboo Gate ask the Buddha how to live well in the home. The Buddha teaches them seven principles they can apply to themselves, which are practical applications of the Golden Rule. Someone with these seven principles and the four factors of stream-entry is a stream-enterer.

name: Four Noble Truths: Abbreviated SN 56 Texts
description: Abbreviated suttas from the Sacca-saṁyutta (SN 56) teaching the Four Noble Truths in condensed form, directed at those with little dust in their eyes.

name: Series & Parts (Thirty)
description: 35 suttas on series, parts, thirty, body (SN dominant)

suttas.title: The Thirty-Two Parts of the Body
suttas.blurb: The thirty-two parts of the body for reflection

suttas.title: 9. Incorporated Abbreviation Series
suttas.blurb: One who does not understand the various factors of dependent origination should seek a teacher.

suttas.title: 1. Abbreviated Texts
suttas.blurb: Five kinds of suffering that only women are subject to, not men.

name: Pasenadi & King (Kosala)
description: 35 suttas on pasenadi, king, kosala, king pasenadi (SN dominant)

suttas.title: With Visākhā
suttas.blurb: The drawbacks of having to do business with kings.

suttas.title: Who Is More Dear?
suttas.blurb: King Pasenadi asks his queen, “Is there anyone dearer to you than yourself?”

suttas.title: King Udena
suttas.blurb: Five hundred awakened women die in a fire.

suttas.title: An Only Son
suttas.blurb: The sorrow that comes from having those who are dear.

suttas.title: Kings
suttas.blurb: When monks have gathered, they shouldn’t spend their time gossiping about kings.

suttas.title: With Visākhā
suttas.blurb: Lady Visākhā wishes for many grandchildren.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: Pasenadi is disappointed to hear that Queen Mallikā has given birth to a daughter. But the Buddha consoles him, saying a woman may turn out better than a man.

suttas.title: Seven Matted-Hair Ascetics
suttas.blurb: How to know another person’s character.

suttas.title: 5. With King Muṇḍa
suttas.blurb: When King Pasenadi learns of the death of his beloved Queen Mallikā, the Buddha consoles him.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: Pasenadi asks the Buddha if there is one thing that secures benefit both in this life and the next.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: Pasenadi loses a battle against his nephew King Ajātasattu of Magadha.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: In a battle subsequent to SN 3.14, Pasenadi defeats Ajātasattu and takes him prisoner. Acting compassionately, he confiscates Ajātasattu’s military assets but releases him alive.

suttas.title: Chapter Four
suttas.blurb: Prince Ajātasattu supplied devadatta with 500 carts of offerings, leading to his downfall.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: In what is probably the world’s first weight loss diet, when Pasenadi shows up overweight and out of breath, the Buddha advises him to cut down his eating. He gives a verse to one of the king’s attendants, and asks him to recite it whenever the king is eating.

suttas.title: 3. Kosala
suttas.blurb: Pasenadi laments the death of his aged grandmother.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: Five kings including Pasenadi are enjoying themselves and wonder which of the senses affords the highest pleasure. They ask the Buddha, who replies that it is subjective, depending on a persons preferences. Inspired, the laymen Candanaṅgalika offers a verse in praise of the Buddha.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: In answer to Pasenadi’s praise of good friendship, the Buddha recounts an earlier conversation with Ānanda on the topic. He then goes on to say that when one has good friends, it is important to develop diligence.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: A diverse group of ascetics passes by, and Pasenadi asks the Buddha if any of them are perfected ones. The Buddha says it is not easy for one such as Pasenadi to judge the spiritual achievements of others. Pasenadi reveals that the men are, in fact, his spies.

suttas.title: 6. Hindrances
suttas.blurb: A mother and son, though ordained as monk and nun, have sexual relations. The Buddha launches a powerful attack on the dangers of sex.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: A wealthy man dies childless, having not enjoyed his riches. The Buddha tells of how in the time of a solitary Buddha of the past named Tagarasikhī, a householder made an offering but later regretted it.

suttas.title: 2. Childless
suttas.blurb: A wealthy man dies childless, having not enjoyed his riches. The Buddha says that wealth should be properly enjoyed and shared.

suttas.title: 3. Kosala
suttas.blurb: “Where should I give” and “Where is a gift is most fruitful” are two different questions.

suttas.title: 20. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Queen Mallikā asks about the karmic causes of beauty, wealth, and power.

suttas.title: 3. Kosala
suttas.blurb: Old age and death roll in upon all like mountains approaching from the four directions, crushing all in their path

suttas.title: 6. Hindrances
suttas.blurb: When some wild young men show deep respect for the Buddha, a family elder is astonished.

suttas.title: 3. Kosala
suttas.blurb: The Buddha teaches Pasenadi of four kinds of person, going from dark to dark, dark to light, light to dark, and light to light.

suttas.title: The Imported Cloth
suttas.blurb: King Pasenadi takes a chance to visit Venerable Ānanda, where he asks about skillful and unskillful behavior, and what is praised by the Buddha. He offers Ānanda a valuable cloth in gratitude.

suttas.title: Born From the Beloved
suttas.blurb: A rare glimpse into the marital life of King Pasenadi, and how he is led to the Dhamma by his Queen, the incomparable Mallikā. She confirms the Buddha’s teaching that our loved ones bring us sorrow; but that’s not something a husband, father, and king wants to hear.

suttas.title: 3. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: King Pasenadi returns victorious from battle and immediately goes to see the Buddha, bowing reverentially to his feet, and declaring ten reasons for his love and devotion to the Buddha.

suttas.title: 5. With King Muṇḍa
suttas.blurb: Depressed by the death of Queen Bhaddā, King Muṇḍa of Rājagaha neglects his duties, and broods by the side of his beloved’s corpse. Venerable Nārada eases his grief.

suttas.title: 3. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Starting with a comparison with the King of Kosala, the Buddha teaches a series of ten advanced teachings. He culminates by affirming that he teaches the full understanding of sensual pleasures, forms, and feelings.

suttas.title: Shrines to the Teaching
suttas.blurb: King Pasenadi, near the end of his life, visits the Buddha, and shows moving devotion and love for his teacher.

suttas.title: The Fruits of the Ascetic Life
suttas.blurb: The newly crowned King Ajātasattu is disturbed by the violent means by which he achieved the crown. He visits the Buddha to find peace of mind, and asks him about the benefits of spiritual practice. This is one of the greatest literary and spiritual texts of early Buddhism.

suttas.title: At Kaṇṇakatthala
suttas.blurb: King Pasenadi questions the Buddha on miscellaneous matters: caste, omniscience, and the gods among them.

suttas.title: About King Maghadeva
suttas.blurb: A rare extended mythic narrative, telling of an ancient kingly lineage and their eventual downfall.

name: Sakka & Gods (Lord)
description: 34 suttas on sakka, gods, lord, demons (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 8. Slain
suttas.blurb: The killing of anger is the only killing praised by the noble ones.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: The Buddha recalls the words of Sakka warning of how anger crushes people like an avalanche

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: The dialogue in SN 40.10 is repeated with a series of other deities.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: Anger is the only thing whose slaying the Buddha commends.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: The god Māgha asks the Buddha about what one should slay in order to sleep well and be without sorrow.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: Sakka reflects that one should not harm even one’s enemy. Knowing this, Vepacitti immediately takes advantage of it.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Sakka and Brahmā Sahampati come to the Buddha’s hut and speak in his praise.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: When Vepacitti fell ill, Sakka visited his old adversary, who begged to be cured. But Sakka demanded to be taught a powerful magic spell first.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: Fleeing the demon host, Sakka’s chariot risks endangering the nests of little birds in the forest. Rather than render the birds homeless, Sakka instructs his charioteer to turn back, even at the cost of his life.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: When two mendicants have a falling out, the Buddha recalls the wise words of Sakka on not letting anger ruin friendship.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: In a parable of a lunar eclipse, the moon god Candimā is swallowed by the demon lord Rāhu. Candimā appeals to the Buddha, who tells Rāhu to let go. This sutta is recited as a protection verse.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: Sakka, lord of gods, and Verocana, lord of demons, approach the Buddha’s hut and speak in praise of effort.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: In days gone by, Sakka, lord of gods, and Vepacitti, lord of demons, visited a hermitage of seers. But Vepacitti behaves rudely, while Sakka honors them. However, the seers beg Sakka to leave, on account of the stink of their unwashed bodies. But he says he loves their smell.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: In a parable of a solar eclipse, the sun god Suriya is swallowed by the demon lord Rāhu. Suriya appeals to the Buddha, who tells Rāhu to let go. This sutta is recited as a protection verse.

suttas.title: 4. Messengers of the Gods
suttas.blurb: One should aspire to be like an awakened mendicant, not like Sakka.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Sakka achieved his status because of seven vows he kept in the past.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: When Sakka sets out on a pleasure jaunt, he begins by lifting his joined palms to the Buddha. His charioteer Mātali points out that Sakka is venerated by gods and men, and asks who he is venerating. Sakka replies that he venerates the Buddha, the perfected mendicants as well as trainees on the path.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: When an ugly spirit takes Sakka’s throne, the gods were outraged. But the more they complained, the prettier he became. Sakka realized this was the so-called “anger eating spirit”, and treated him with kindness and respect.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: When Sakka sets out on a pleasure jaunt, he begins by lifting his joined palms to the four quarters . His charioteer Mātali points out that Sakka is venerated by gods and men, and asks who he is venerating. Sakka replies that he venerates both well-practiced mendicants as well as virtuous lay folk.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: When Sakka, the Lord of Gods, tries to organize a conterattack against the demons, he is frustrated by his lazy general Susīma. Sakka asks Susīma how one might get what one wants without effort; but Susīma apparently believes Sakka can grant any wish he wants. The Buddha emphasizes that if effort is valuable among the gods, how much more so for mendicants.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna visits the gods of the Thirty-Three and encourages them in the four factors of stream-entry.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: In the past, when battle between the gods and demons loomed, some seers sought out the demon lord Sambara to beg for protection. But due to their devotion to Sakka, he rejected them, so they cursed him.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: When Sakka sets out on a pleasure jaunt, he begins by lifting his joined palms to the Saṅgha of mendicants. His charioteer Mātal asks why Sakka venerates smelly mendicants. Sakka replies that he venerates them for their renunciation and peacefulness.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: When Sakka, the Lord of Gods, tries to organize a counterattack against the demons, he is frustrated by his lazy general Suvīra. Sakka asks Suvīra how one might get what one wants without effort; but Suvīra apparently believes Sakka can grant any wish he wants. The Buddha emphasizes that if effort is valuable among the gods, how much more so for mendicants.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: The gods complain when a poor man is reborn in heaven. But Sakka points out that even though poor, he did many good deeds.

suttas.title: 4. Deities
suttas.blurb: Mahāmoggallāna is visited by a Brahmā god who had formerly been a mendicant, and they discuss which of the gods are stream-enterers.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Sakka’s many names arose due to the diverse good deeds he did in the past.

suttas.title: About Nālaka
suttas.blurb: A sutta in two parts. The first part gives an account of events soon after the birth of the Bodhisatta. The second part describes the way of the sage.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: The demon lord Vepacitti proposes to Sakka that they engage in a battle of wits in debate rather than war. Vepacitti speaks in praise of forceful punishment, while Sakka stands up for patience and forgiveness.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: In a battle between the gods and the demons, and the demon lord Vepacitti is captured and brought in chains to Sakka’s hall. Vepacitti abuses Sakka, who endures his harsh words in patience. The charioteer Mātali asks whether he is silent out of weakness or fear, but Sakka says there is no point is wasting words arguing with a fool.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: Sakka rallies his troops with the sight of his banner. But the Buddha points out that the deities, powerful as they are, are not free of fear, but he is. He encourages the mendicants to recollect the Triple Gem to abandon fear.

suttas.title: 4. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: In the wars between gods and demons, they find safety only in their own fortress. Similarly, a mendicant finds safety in absorption.

suttas.title: 6. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: When someone is angry, they wish ill upon their enemy, and are disappointed if they do well. This sutta includes a powerful set of verses.

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna ascended to the heaven of the Thirty-Three to address Sakka, the lord of gods, and his extensive retinue. He spoke in praise of going for refuge, as it leads to heaven. Sakka affirmed this on multiple occasions, adding that gods who had gone for refuge and kept ethics surpassed the other deities in the heavens.

name: Faculties & Plain Version (Plain)
description: 33 suttas on faculties, plain version, plain, version (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Three faculties: femininity, masculinity, and vitality.

suttas.title: 8. Bitable
suttas.blurb: Only a noble disciple truly understand the five aggregates’ origin, ending, gratification, drawback, and escape.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Six faculties: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: The five faculties are faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.

suttas.title: 8. Bitable
suttas.blurb: Only a noble disciple truly understand the five aggregates’ gratification, drawback, and escape.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Three faculties relating to stages in the realization of awakening.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: One who understands the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 4. The Pleasure Faculty
suttas.blurb: One who understands the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the five faculties of pleasure, pain, happiness, sadness, and equanimity is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: One who understands the origin, the passing, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 8. Bitable
suttas.blurb: Only a noble disciple truly understand the five aggregates’ origin, ending, gratification, drawback, and escape.

suttas.title: 11. Ends
suttas.blurb: One who truly understand these five aggregates is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Six faculties: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. One who understand these is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: One who is freed after understanding the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a perfected one.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: One who is freed after understanding the origin, the passing, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the five faculties is a perfected one.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Six faculties: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind. One who is freed by understanding these is perfected.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: Each of the five faculties should be seen in a particular set of teachings.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: Depending on the strength of the five faculties, one is perfected, or reaches a lesser attainment.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: The Buddha did not claim to be awakened until he realized the five faculties.

suttas.title: 3. The Six Faculties
suttas.blurb: The Buddha did not claim to be awakened until he realized the origin, the passing, the gratification, the drawback, and the escape regarding the six faculties.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: Having developed energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom a mendicant may declare their awakening.

suttas.title: 15. The Old and the New
suttas.blurb: One is accomplished in the sense faculties by being freed from attachment to them.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: Having developed faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom a mendicant may declare their awakening.

suttas.title: 7. Pairs
suttas.blurb: Stream-enterers may realize the final goal in this life or the next.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: Having developed wisdom and immersion a mendicant may declare their awakening.

suttas.title: 7. Pairs
suttas.blurb: Those who have certainty in the Buddha may realize the final goal in this life or the next.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: Having developed wisdom a mendicant may declare their awakening, as wisdom fulfills the other faculties.

suttas.title: 2. Weaker
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains the five faculties in terms of how they are obtained.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a short explanation of each of the five faculties.

suttas.title: 16. Faculties
suttas.blurb: Things that lead to the disappearance of the Dharma.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: The brahmin Uṇṇābha points out that the five physical sense faculties do not share a common domain, so where do they come together? In the mind. Uṇṇābha pursues the teaching all the way to Nibbāna.

suttas.title: 15. The Old and the New
suttas.blurb: How is it that a mendicant can declare their awakening? By understanding whether any desire arises from sense stimuli.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: The Buddha discusses the five faculties and the five powers. They are like a river that flows around a central island. From one point of view they can be considered different, but from another they are just the same stream.

suttas.title: 1. Plain Version
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a detailed explanation of each of the five faculties.

name: Overflowing Merit & Overflowing (Merit)
description: 32 suttas on overflowing merit, overflowing, merit, merit overflowing (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 6. Overflowing Merit
suttas.blurb: Long life, beauty, happiness, and strength.

suttas.title: 6. Overflowing Merit
suttas.blurb: How a married couple can stay together in this life and the next.

suttas.title: Blessings
suttas.blurb: A list of the types of skillful behavior that give blessings and protection.

suttas.title: 5. Overflowing Merit, With Verses
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple with the four factors of stream-entry is a stream-enterer.

suttas.title: 5. Overflowing Merit, With Verses

suttas.blurb: A mendicant should thoroughly investigate the causes of suffering in accordance with dependent origination. If someone who still has ignorance makes a choice, their consciousness fares on to a suitable state of existence. But one who has eradicated ignorance is detached and is not reborn anywhere.

suttas.title: 1. Ignorance
suttas.blurb: Ānanda sees the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi resplendent on his all-white chariot. He asks the Buddha whether there is a similarly divine vehicle in Buddhism. The Buddha responds by drawing a detailed set of analogies between the eightfold path and a chariot.

suttas.title: 1. Ignorance
suttas.blurb: The Buddha presents the eightfold path together with a detailed analysis of each factor. It should be assumed that these explanations apply wherever the eightfold path is taught.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a parable of a large log floating downstream, which, if it steers clear of the many obstacles, will eventually reach the ocean. Venerable Kimbila asks for an explanation, and the Buddha clarifies each item.

suttas.title: 1. Similes
suttas.blurb: Only a speedster could catch arrows in flight, yet conditioned phenomena perish faster than that.

suttas.title: 7. Fitting Deeds

suttas.title: 1. Similes
suttas.blurb: As an ancient drum has disintegrated, so too will the true teachings disappear.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: A viper bit Venerable Upasena, but he remained poised, declaring that he had no identification with the six senses.

suttas.title: 1. Similes
suttas.blurb: A mendicant who overly associates with the laity is like an elephant calf who tries to copy the great bull elephants.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: A parable of a jackal who fails to eat a tortoise who stays still, with limbs retracted, like a mendicant guarding the senses.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: When a mendicant has three qualities they live happily: sense restraint, moderation in eating, and wakefulness.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: A mendicant goes to a series of teachers and asks how vision is purified. Dissatisfied with all their answers, he complains to the Buddha, who illustrates his quandary with the famous simile of the Kiṁsuka tree. Just as the same Kiṁsuka tree might be described differently by people focusing on the leaves, or the fruit, or the bark, etc., so too the various teachers each speak of the aspect of Dhamma most meaningful to them.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a parable of a large log floating downstream, which, if it steers clear of the many obstacles, will eventually reach the ocean. A mendicant asks for an explanation, and the Buddha clarifies each item. A cowherd named Nanda was listening, and determined to go forth.

suttas.title: The Simile of the Cloth
suttas.blurb: The many different kinds of impurities that defile the mind are compared to a dirty cloth. When the mind is clean we find joy, which leads to states of higher consciousness. Finally, the Buddha rejects the Brahmanical notion that purity comes from bathing in sacred rivers.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: One should restrain the senses like a farmer watching over a field. The Buddha gives the parable of a man bewitched when he first hears a lute. He takes apart the instrument in search of the sound, but is disillusioned when no sound is found.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha strings a series of powerful similes—vipers, murderers, an empty village, and more—into an extended parable of the dangers of existence and the way of escape.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The six senses are like a sheaf of barley struck with six flails; and the desire for rebirth is a seventh flail. The Buddha goes on to speak of the cunning trap of the gods; but the trap of Māra, conceit, is even more subtle.

suttas.title: The Simile of the Saw
suttas.blurb: A discourse full of vibrant and memorable similes, on the importance of patience and love even when faced with abuse and criticism. The Buddha finishes with the simile of the saw, one of the most memorable similes found in the discourses.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The senses are like a snake, a crocodile, a bird, a dog, a jackal, and a monkey all tied up together, pulling this way and that. Mindfulness is like a post that keeps them grounded.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha defines the things that entail suffering as the five aggregates, and urges mendicants to be free of desire for the six senses, giving a series of vivid similes. He then speaks of how even for a mendicant practicing restraint a lapse of mindfulness might occur from time to time.

suttas.title: 19. The Simile of the Vipers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is invited to teach in a new hall in Kapilavatthu. Late at night, after teaching the Sakyans, the Buddha invites Moggallāna to teach. He speaks on the mental corruption that flows from attachment to the senses.

suttas.title: The Simile of the Quail
suttas.blurb: Again raising the rule regarding eating, but this time as a reflection of gratitude for the Buddha in eliminating things that cause complexity and stress. The Buddha emphasizes how attachment even to little things can be dangerous.

suttas.title: The Longer Simile of the Elephant’s Footprint
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta gives an elaborate demonstration of how, just as any footprint can fit inside an elephant’s, all the Buddha’s teaching can fit inside the four noble truths. This offers an overall template for organizing the Buddha’s teachings.

suttas.title: The Simile of the Cobra
suttas.blurb: One of the monks denies that prohibited conduct is really a problem. The monks and then the Buddha subject him to an impressive dressing down. The Buddha compares someone who understands only the letter of the teachings to someone who grabs a snake by the tail, and also invokes the famous simile of the raft.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: Kassapa asks the Buddha why there are now more rules but fewer awakened mendicants. The Buddha replies that it is due to the arising of the counterfeit Dhamma, giving five factors that lead to decline

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta approaches Kassapa and asks how it is that only someone who is keen and conscientious can realize freedom.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: Venerable Kāmabhū asks Citta to explain an enigmatic verse on a “one-spoked chariot”. After a brief consideration, Citta is able to explain it in detail.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha invites Kassapa to teach the mendicants, but he is reluctant, saying that the mendicants have become stubborn and their good qualities are declining.

suttas.title: 13. Kusinārā
suttas.blurb: The Buddha discusses different kinds of teachers while staying at a hermitage near his home town of Kapilavatthu.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: Some mendicants approach families in the hope of receiving fine gifts or respect—but not Kassapa.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha invites Kassapa to teach the mendicants, but he is reluctant, since certain students of Anuruddha and Ānanda have been competing to see who can teach better.

suttas.title: 3. Various Sectarians
suttas.blurb: A series of deities who had formerly been ascetics in the non-Buddhist sects came to the Buddha, speaking verses in praise—or occasional criticism—of their former teachers

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: When Citta was on his deathbed, rather than receiving comfort, he gave comfort and teaching to those present.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: Kassapa approaches families like the moon, with humility, keeping his distance, and not getting involved. So when he teaches, it is with pure intentions.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: Citta the householder invites some mendicants to his home for a meal. When they left he followed them, and witnessed the junior monk Venerable Mahaka performing a psychic feat. Citta approached Mahaka in his hut and asked him to demonstrate his skills, and Mahaka obliged. Citta offers to support him with requisites, but Mahaka left, never to return.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: When Kassapa is persuaded by Ānanda to go with him to teach the nuns, the nun Thullatissā compares him unfavorably to Ānanda, prompting Kassapa to issue a warning and make a bold claim of his spiritual mastery. This discourse appears to be set after the Buddha’s passing.

suttas.title: 9. Ascetics
suttas.blurb: A monk is critical of the Buddha, but later feels remorse.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: The Jain leader Mahāvīra, known as Nigaṇṭha Nātaputa in the Buddhist tradition, is visited by Citta. Mahāvīra asks him whether he believes in a certain state of immersion. When Citta replies that he doesn’t go by faith in the Buddha on this point, Mahāvīra is delighted. But it turns out Citta doesn’t need faith, as he experiences such a state for himself.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: Citta meets with the naked ascetic Kassapa, an old friend of his, and asks what gains he has realized from the spiritual life. Kassapa admits he has realized no higher attainments, whereupon Citta reveals he has realized many such attainments. Kassapa decides to ordain as a Buddhist mendicant.

suttas.title: 10. With Kakudha
suttas.blurb: Kakudha, Venerable Moggallāna’s lay supporter, having recently passed away, returned to warn him about Devadatta’s fall. The Buddha analyses different kinds of teacher.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: Citta asks Kāmabhū about the different kinds of processes (saṅkhāra) in a series of questions that lead to the most profound of meditation experiences.

suttas.title: 1. With Citta
suttas.blurb: Venerable Godatta asks Citta whether the liberations of measurelessness, nothingness, emptiness, and signlessness are different states, or just different words for the same thing. Citta explains that they are both: they are terms for different meditation experiences, but may also be used of perfection or arahantship.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha asserts that just as he has full mastery of all the meditation attainments and higher spiritual realizations, so does Kassapa.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: When several of Ānanda’s students disrobe, Kassapa admonishes him, calling him “boy”. The nun Thullanandā hears of this and criticizes Kassapa, claiming he formerly followed another teacher. But Kassapa refutes this, and gives an account of his going forth and first encounter with the Buddha.

suttas.title: With Moggallāna the Guardian
suttas.blurb: Amid rising military tensions after the Buddha’s death, Venerable Ānanda is questioned about how the Saṅgha planned to continue in their teacher’s absence. As the Buddha refused to appoint a successor, the teaching and practice that he laid down become the teacher, and the Saṅgha resolves issues by consensus.

suttas.title: A Trainee
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is invited by his family, the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu, to inaugurate a new community hall. He invites Venerable Ānanda to explain in detail the stages of spiritual practice for a lay trainee.

suttas.blurb: Using the “object lesson” of a cup of water, the Buddha explains to his son, Rāhula, the importance of telling the truth and reflecting on one’s motives.

suttas.title: 12. The World and the Kinds of Sensual Stimulation
suttas.blurb: Realizing that his son Rāhula was ripe for awakening, the Buddha takes him for a personal teaching. He questions Rāhula on the impermanence of all the factors of sense experience, leading Rāhula to realize full freedom.

suttas.title: The Longer Advice to Rāhula
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells Rāhula to meditate on not-self, which he immediately puts into practice. Seeing him, Venerable Sāriputta advises him to develop breath meditation, but the Buddha suggests a wide range of different practices first.

suttas.title: The Shorter Advice to Rāhula
suttas.blurb: The Buddha takes Rāhula with him to a secluded spot in order to lead him on to liberation.

suttas.title: 1. At the Cāpāla Shrine

name: Great & Fine (Night)
description: 25 suttas on great, fine, night, wanderers (AN dominant)

suttas.title: 5. With Rohitassa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha praises the teaching of the monk Visākha.

suttas.title: 1. Monks
suttas.blurb: The Buddha praises Venerable Visākha Pañcāliputta for teaching the Dhamma.

suttas.title: 7. Pairs
suttas.blurb: The monks are sitting around chatting about this and that. The Buddha admonishes them and teaches ten topics of conversation suitable for mendicants.

suttas.title: 7. Pairs
suttas.blurb: The monks are sitting around chatting about this and that. The Buddha admonishes them and teaches ten grounds of praise.

suttas.title: 3. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Some wanderers claim to teach the direct knowledge of all things, just like the Buddha. However the Buddha disputes this by presenting ten “Great Questions” that they cannot answer.

suttas.title: 3. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Some lay people ask the nun from Kajaṅgalā regarding the ten “Great Questions”. Though she has not heard this teaching herself, she explains from her own understanding, and is praised by the Buddha.

suttas.title: 3. At Uruvelā
suttas.blurb: A discussion with some wanderers on the basic principles.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Venerable Lomasavaṅgīsa explains to Mahānāma that the difference between a trainee and the Realized One is that the trainees practice to give up the hindrances, whereas the Realized One has already ended all defilements.

suttas.title: 6. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Several senior mendicants discuss the “Questions of Metteyya” from the Pārāyana (Snp 5.3). Each propose an interpretation, and they then ask the Buddha. He praises each reading, but gives his own intended meaning.

suttas.title: Ānanda and One Fine Night
suttas.blurb: The same discourse as MN 131, but spoken by Venerable Ānanda.

suttas.title: Lomasakaṅgiya and One Fine Night
suttas.blurb: A monk who does not know the verses from MN 131 is encouraged by a deity to learn them.

suttas.title: 6. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: The junior monk Citta Hatthisāriputta rudely interrupts his seniors, and is admonished by Mahākoṭṭhita. His friends speak in his defense, but Mahākoṭṭhita warns them with a long explanation of how hard it is to know another’s mind. Ultimately, Citta disrobes.

suttas.title: 6. Discussion
suttas.blurb: Some wanderers tell some Buddhist mendicants that they, too, teach the five hindrances and the seven awakening factors, so what is the difference? The Buddha explains by giving a detailed analytical treatment that he says is beyond the scope of the wanderers.

suttas.title: 2. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: General Sīha, a prominent disciple of the Jains, visits the Buddha against the wishes of Mahāvīra, the Jain leader. He challenges the Buddha by bringing up the various ways that people criticize him. Convinced by the Buddha’s response, Sīha wishes to take refuge in the Buddha; but the Buddha urges him to be cautious and to continue supporting the Jains.

suttas.title: One Fine Night
suttas.blurb: This discourse opens with a short but powerful set of verses extolling the benefits of insight into the here and now, followed by an explanation.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Susı̄ma fraudulently enters the Sangha so that the wanderers can learn the secret to the Buddha’s fame and success. Hearing that some mendicants claimed to be perfected, Susīma approaches and asks them if they have developed psychic powers or the formless states, to which they say no. Perplexed, he asks the Buddha, who explains that awakening follows insight into the principles of impermanence and causality. Eventually Susı̄ma confesses his misdeed.

suttas.title: At Sāmagāma
suttas.blurb: Hearing of the death of the Jain leader Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, the Buddha encourages the Saṅgha to swiftly resolve any disputes. He lays down a series of seven methods for resolving disputes. These form the foundation for the monastic code.

suttas.title: With Sunakkhatta
suttas.blurb: Not all of those who claim to be awakened are genuine. The Buddha teaches how true spiritual progress depends on an irreversible letting go of the forces that lead to suffering.

suttas.title: With Sandaka
suttas.blurb: Venerable Ānanda teaches a group of wanderers how there are many different approaches to the spiritual life, many of which lead nowhere.

suttas.title: The Great Discourse on the Buddha’s Extinguishment
suttas.blurb: The longest of all discourses, this extended narrative tells of the events surrounding the Buddha’s death. Full of vivid and moving details, it is an ideal entry point into knowing the Buddha as a person, and understanding how the Buddhist community coped with his passing.

suttas.title: Mahākaccāna and One Fine Night
suttas.blurb: The verses from MN 131 are explained in a different way by Venerable Mahakaccāna.

suttas.title: An Impressive Discourse
suttas.blurb: Following the death of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, the leader of the Jains, the Buddha emphasizes the stability and maturity of his own community. He encourages the community to come together after his death and recite the teachings in harmony.

suttas.title: The Great Discourse on Traces Left Behind
suttas.blurb: The Buddha teaches about the six Buddhas of the past, and tells a lengthy account of one of those, Vipassī.

suttas.title: The Marks of a Great Man
suttas.blurb: This presents the brahmanical prophecy of the Great Man, and explains the 32 marks in detail. This discourse contains some of the latest and most complex verse forms in the canon.

suttas.title: The Divine Net
suttas.blurb: While others may praise or criticize the Buddha, they tend to focus on trivial details. The Buddha presents an analysis of 62 kinds of wrong view, seeing through which one becomes detached from meaningless speculations.

suttas.title: The Wheel-Turning Monarch
suttas.blurb: In illustration of his dictum that one should rely on oneself, the Buddha gives a detailed account of the fall of a kingly lineage of the past, and the subsequent degeneration of society. This process, however, is not over, as the Buddha predicts that eventually society will fall into utter chaos. But far in the future, another Buddha, Metteyya, will arise in a time of peace and plenty.

suttas.blurb: When a lion emerges from its lair and roars, animals are terrified. Similarly, when the Buddha teaches of the impermanence of the aggregates, even the gods are filled with fear. This is illustrated with a set of verses.

suttas.title: 2. The Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: Venerables Mahākoṭṭhita and Sāriputta discuss whether the spiritual path is for the purpose of changing the results of deeds.

suttas.title: 2. The Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta visits some wanderers, who claim that only perfected ones are free from bad rebirth. Sāriputta has no opinion on this, but asks the Buddha, who replies that even stream-enterers are freed from lower rebirth.

suttas.title: 2. The Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: The wealthy and devoted lay supporter Anāthapiṇḍika rather curiously says that only poor alms are given in his home. The Buddha praises gracious and bounteous generosity. But meditation surpasses even the greatest offering.

suttas.title: 3. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Like a lion, a Realized One roars his preeminence based on ten powers.

suttas.title: 6. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: The Buddha possesses six spiritual powers that enable him to roar his lion’s roar.

suttas.title: 2. The Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: After the rains season, Sāriputta takes leave of the Buddha. Another monk falsely accuses Sāriputta of hitting him before leaving. The Buddha calls Sāriputta to answer the accusation. Before the Saṅgha, Sāriputta denies it, uttering a powerful statement of spiritual prowess.

suttas.title: The Ascetic Who Behaved Like a Dog
suttas.blurb: Some ascetics in ancient India undertook extreme practices, such as a vow to behave like an ox or a dog. The Buddha meets two such individuals, and is reluctantly pressed to reveal the kammic outcomes of such practice.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: A disrobed monk, Sunakkhatta, attacks the Buddha’s teaching because it merely leads to the end of suffering. The Buddha counters that this is, in fact, praise, and goes on to enumerate his many profound and powerful achievements.

suttas.title: The Lion’s Roar to the Naked Ascetic Kassapa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is challenged by a naked ascetic on the topic of spiritual austerities. He points out that it is quite possible to perform all kinds of austere practices without having any inner purity of mind.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse on the Lion’s Roar
suttas.blurb: The Buddha declares that only those following his path can genuinely experience the four stages of awakening. This is because, while much is shared with other systems, none of them go so far as to fully reject all attachment to the idea of a self.

suttas.title: The Lion’s Roar at the Monastery of Lady Udumbarikā
suttas.blurb: This discourse gives a specially good example of dialog between religions. The Buddha insists that he is not interested to make anyone give up their teacher or practices, but only to help people let go of suffering.

suttas.title: 1. Fools

suttas.title: With Pasūra

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse on Arrayed For Battle
suttas.blurb: If the truth is one, how should a person behave in a world where many different truths are taught?

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse on Arrayed for Battle
suttas.blurb: How to maintain freedom in a world full of disputes.

suttas.title: The Foolish and the Astute
suttas.blurb: A fool suffers both in this life and the next, while the astute benefits in both respects.

suttas.blurb: Noble and ignoble searches.

suttas.title: 13. Searches
suttas.blurb: Develop the eightfold path to abandon the three cravings. This sutta is doubled by replacing taṇhā with another form of the same word, tasina. This variation is found in Burmese but not Sinhalese manuscripts.

name: Precedes & Precedes Sun (Dawn Precedes)

suttas.title: 6. Abbreviated Texts on the Sun
suttas.blurb: As the dawn precedes the sun, good friendship precedes the eightfold path.

suttas.title: 8. If You Want

suttas.title: 6. Abbreviated Texts on the Sun
suttas.blurb: As the dawn precedes the sun, proper attention precedes the eightfold path.

suttas.title: 3. Abodes of Sentient Beings
suttas.blurb: Nine kinds of horses are compared with nine kinds of monks.

suttas.title: 2. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Eight flaws in wild horses, similar to eight flaws in mendicants.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: When a king’s fortress is well supplied with seven things and has four kinds of food supplies it is impregnable. Similarly, a noble disciple with seven qualities and the four absorptions is impregnable.

name: Wisdom & Great (Wise)
description: 20 suttas on wisdom, great, wise, entry wise (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 6. A Wise Person
suttas.blurb: Association with good people, hearing the teaching, proper attention, and right practice lead to the fruit of stream-entry.

name: Channa & Ānanda (Retreat)
description: 20 suttas on channa, ānanda, retreat, sense experience (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 8. Sick
suttas.blurb: Venerable Rādha asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha told him to abandon desire for sense experience, which is not-self.

suttas.title: 8. Sick
suttas.blurb: Venerable Rādha asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha told him to abandon desire for sense experience, which is suffering.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Samiddhi asks the Buddha what suffering is. The Buddha says that suffering is present so long as the senses exist.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Samiddhi asks the Buddha what a “sentient being” is. The Buddha says that a sentient being is present so long as the senses exist.

suttas.title: 2. Impermanence
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha what exactly is meant when we say that things cease. The Buddha explains it as the cessation of the aggregates.

suttas.title: 16. The End of Relishing
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha says to give up desire for what is not-self, i.e. the process of sense experience.

suttas.title: 9. With Channa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells Ānanda that the “world” consisting of six sense experience is empty because it is empty of self and what belongs to self.

suttas.title: 8. Sick
suttas.blurb: Venerable Rādha asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha told him to abandon desire for sense experience, which is impermanent.

suttas.title: 16. The End of Relishing
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha says to give up desire for what is suffering, i.e. the process of sense experience.

suttas.title: 16. The End of Relishing
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha says to give up desire for what is impermanent, i.e. the process of sense experience.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Samiddhi asks the Buddha what the world is. The Buddha says that the world is present so long as the senses exist.

suttas.title: 9. With Channa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells Ānanda that in the training of the noble one the “world” consisting of six sense experience is liable to wear out.

suttas.title: 15. Views
suttas.blurb: When Ānanda wants to go on retreat, the Buddha advises him to contemplate the aggregates

suttas.title: 8. Ānanda
suttas.blurb: How consciousness, karma, and craving create and sustain future lives.

suttas.title: 4. It’s Not Yours
suttas.blurb: The Buddha speaks to Ānanda on what at AN 3.47 described as the “characteristics of conditioned phenomena”: arising, ceasing, and change while persisting. This was to become a critical aspect of later theories on the nature of change. Ānanda explains in terms of the aggregates.

suttas.title: 8. Ānanda
suttas.blurb: How consciousness, karma, and craving create and sustain future lives, with an emphasis on intention.

suttas.title: 9. With Channa
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha teaches him that the senses are impermanent, etc.

suttas.title: 9. With Channa
suttas.blurb: Venerable Bāhiya asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha teaches him that the senses are impermanent, etc.

suttas.title: 4. It’s Not Yours
suttas.blurb: The Buddha speaks to Ānanda on what at AN 3.47 described as the “characteristics of conditioned phenomena”: arising, ceasing, and change while persisting, as applied to past, future, and present. Ānanda explains in terms of the aggregates.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Araka was a famous teacher long ago, when the life span was much greater than today. Nevertheless, he still taught impermanence; how much more is it relevant today!

name: Undeclared Points & Undeclared (Points)
description: 20 suttas on undeclared points, undeclared, points, points undeclared (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 18. Intention
suttas.blurb: When the senses end, is there anything left? Or is that just proliferation?

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks Sāriputta whether a Realized One exists after death. Sāriputta says that only someone who has desire for the five aggregates would think in such terms.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Vacchagotta asks the Buddha whether a self exists, but the Buddha is silent. When Ānanda later asks him why he didn’t answer, the Buddha replied that it was to avoid misunderstanding.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks Sāriputta whether a Realized One exists after death. Sāriputta says that one who understands the five aggregates would not think in such terms.

suttas.title: 1. Kassapa
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks Kassapa about whether a Realized One survives death.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Vacchagotta asks Venerable Sabhiya Kaccāna whether a Realized One exists after death. He answers that the basis for describing a Realized One no longer applies. Vacchagotta is impressed that such a junior monk understands this subtle matter.

suttas.title: 18. Intention
suttas.blurb: When the senses end, is there anything left? Or is that just proliferation?

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Mahākoṭṭhita asks Sāriputta whether a Realized One exists after death. Sāriputta says that to answer this would be to get involved in the five aggregates, something that a Realized One is free from.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Vacchagotta asks the Buddha the ten metaphysical questions, starting with whether the cosmos is eternal. He says such questions would only be answered by someone who identifies with the five aggregates.

suttas.title: 6. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains why a noble disciple has no doubt when it comes to the unexplained points.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Vacchagotta asks Moggallāna the ten metaphysical questions, starting with whether the cosmos is eternal. Moggallāna says such questions would only be answered by someone who identifies with the six senses. Vacchagotta then asks the Buddha the same questions.

suttas.title: 6. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains the benefits of giving that are visible in the present life, and one that is only apparent in the next.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Vacchagotta reports to the Buddha a conversation among ascetics on the views of the six heretical teachers as to where a perfected one is reborn. Unsure of what the Buddha’s position was, he asks how it is to be understood. The Buddha says it is like a flame that burns dependent on fuel, and goes out when that fuel is extinguished.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks Mahākoṭṭhita whether a Realized One exists after death. Mahākoṭṭhita says that only someone who delights in the five aggregates would think in such terms, and offers a variety of further explanations of the same point.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: While staying in Toraṇavatthu, King Pasenadi wishes to visit a spiritual teacher, and the nun Khemā is highly recommended to him. He asks her about whether a realized one still exists after death, and she says this is not answerable. Later he visits the Buddha, who replies in exactly the same way.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Venerables Mahākoṭṭhita and Sāriputta discuss whether the factors of dependent origination are created by oneself, another, both, or by chance.

suttas.title: 6. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Two gods come to the Buddha on Vulture’s Peak and declare that the nuns are liberated. Moggallāna interrogates one of the deities on what he means, and the Buddha adds further explanation.

suttas.title: 1. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Venerable Anurādha is questioned by a number of ascetics, and ends up by saying that a realized one is described in terms other than “still existing after death” and so on. The wanderers say he’s a fool, so he checks with the Buddha, who says that a realized one is not even apprehended in this life, so how can they be described after death?

suttas.title: With Vacchagotta on Fire
suttas.blurb: Refusing to take a stance regarding useless metaphysical speculations, the Buddha illustrates the spiritual goal with the simile of a flame going out.

suttas.title: The Great Elaboration
suttas.blurb: A series of questions and answers between Sāriputta and Mahākoṭṭhita, examining various subtle and abstruse aspects of the teachings.

suttas.title: 1. Lifespan

suttas.title: 3. Māra
suttas.blurb: Māra repeatedly pesters the monk Samiddhi when he is on retreat. The Buddha encourages him, until eventually Samiddhi is able to recognize Māra for himself.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Samiddhi asks the Buddha what Māra is. The Buddha says that Māra is present so long as the senses exist.

suttas.title: Striving
suttas.blurb: Māra attempts to dissuade the Bodhisatta from his path.

suttas.title: 3. Māra
suttas.blurb: Māra approaches some young mendicants as an old brahmin, and tries to tempt them with talk of the pleasures of youth.

suttas.title: 3. Māra
suttas.blurb: Māra has tried to distract the Buddha for seven years without success. He laments his failure with the similes of a crab whose limbs are smashed by a group of children, and a crow who tried to eat a stone.

suttas.title: 3. Māra
suttas.blurb: Venerable Godhika finds himself repeatedly falling away from his meditation attainments due to severe illness. Eventually he considers suicide, and Māra tries to get the Buddha to deter him. But the Buddha says Godhika has already attained awakening and has died blamelessly.

suttas.title: Sowing
suttas.blurb: The Buddha compares getting trapped by Māra with a deer getting caught in a snare, illustrating the ever more complex strategies employed by hunter and hunted.

suttas.title: 3. Māra
suttas.blurb: Depressed, Māra laments to his three daughters of his failure to distract the Buddha. So they take on the task themselves, and assume a variety of sensuous forms to tempt him. But they fail too, and Māra castigates them for being so presumptuous

suttas.title: The Condemnation of Māra
suttas.blurb: Māra, the trickster and god of death, tried to annoy Moggallāna. He not only failed but was subject to a stern sermon warning of the dangers of attacking the Buddha’s disciples.

suttas.blurb: The famous first discourse, taught at Varanasi to the group of five ascetics. It begins by rejecting the extremes of asceticism and indulgence and recommends the middle way of the eightfold path. Then it defines the four noble truths and analyzes them in twelve aspects. It ends with Venerable Kondañña becoming the first person apart from the Buddha to realize the Dhamma.

suttas.title: The Analysis of the Truths

suttas.title: The Many Kinds of Feeling
suttas.blurb: The Buddha resolves a disagreement on the number of kinds of feelings that he taught, pointing out that different ways of teaching are appropriate in different contexts, and should not be a cause of disputes. He goes on to show the importance of pleasure in developing higher meditation.

suttas.blurb: A mendicant asks whether anything in the aggregates has even the tiniest bit of stability or permanence. The Buddha answers using the simile of a little dirt under his fingernail.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha doesn’t dispute with the world; the world disputes with him. He has understood the five aggregates and explains them. Like a lotus, he was born in the swamp, but rises above it.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: Nothing in the aggregates has even the tiniest bit of stability or permanence. In a past life, the Buddha was a great king with vast properties, but all those conditions have passed away.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: Contemplating the aggregates leads to liberation, but this may not be immediately apparent. The Buddha illustrates this with similes of a chook sitting on eggs, the wearing away of an axe handle, and the rotting of a ship’s rigging.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a series of similes for the aggregates: physical form is like foam, feeling is like a bubble, perception is like a mirage, choices are like a coreless tree, and consciousness is like an illusion.

suttas.title: 8. Bitable
suttas.blurb: One of the most extensive discourses in this collection begins with the Buddha saying that when anyone recollects a past life, all they are recollecting is the five aggregates. He then gives a distinctive set of definitions of the aggregates in terms of their functions, and discusses them from various aspects.

suttas.title: With Māgaṇḍiya
suttas.blurb: Māgandiya offers the Buddha his daughter in marriage. The Buddha refuses and further subdues Māgandiya’s pride by describing a state of peace that Māgandiya doesn’t understand.

suttas.title: Chapter Three
suttas.blurb: A laywoman should wish that if her son remains in the household life, he should be like Citta and Hatthaka. But if he becomes a monk, may material possessions, honor, and praise not come to him!

suttas.blurb: A laywoman should wish that if her daughter remains in the household life, she should be like Khujjuttarā and Veḷukaṇḍakiyā. But if she becomes a nun, may material possessions, honor, and praise not come to her!

suttas.title: 3. Householders
suttas.blurb: The Buddha celebrates the seven amazing qualities of the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī. When he hears of this, Hatthaka is anxious to know that no lay people were present. Learning of this, the Buddha adds humility as an eight quality.

suttas.title: 3. Householders

suttas.title: 1. Benefits
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks Sāriputta about a mysterious state of immersion in meditation where there is no normal perception, yet one is still conscious. Sāriputta confirms that there is, and claims to have attained it.

suttas.title: 3. The Ten Powers
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta is asked by Venerable Bhūmija as to the origin of pleasure and pain. He replies that the Buddha teaches that pleasure and pain originate by conditions. Moreover, all those who offer opinions on this question are themselves part of the web of conditions, as they cannot state their views without contact. Ānanda reports the exchange to the Buddha, who praises Sāriputta’s answer.

suttas.title: 12. The World and the Kinds of Sensual Stimulation
suttas.blurb: A mendicant informs Sāriputta that one of his friends had disrobed. Sāriputta attributes this to lack of sense restraint, eating too much, and not being wakeful.

suttas.title: 4. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta declares that extinguishment is bliss, precisely because nothing is felt there. He explains this apparent paradox with reference to the nine progressive meditations.

suttas.blurb: Prince Abhaya reports Pūraṇa Kassapa’s view that there is no cause for ignorance or knowledge, and asks the Buddha’s opinion. The Buddha replies that the cause of ignorance is the hindrances, and the cause of knowledge is the awakening factors.

suttas.title: To Vacchagotta on the Three Knowledges
suttas.blurb: The Buddha denies being omniscient, and sets forth the three higher knowledges that form the core of his awakened insight.

name: Private & Anuruddha (Mindfulness Meditation)
description: 18 suttas on private, anuruddha, mindfulness meditation, private private (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: The four kinds of mindfulness meditation lead to the ending of craving.

suttas.title: 3. Ethics and Duration
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks Anuruddha what makes someone an adept. It is someone who has fully developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta and Moggallāna ask Anuruddha what an adept mendicant should practice. He replies that it is the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 3. Ethics and Duration
suttas.blurb: Anuruddha claims to have developed the various direct knowledges by practicing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: When Sāriputta ask Anuruddha how he attained such higher knowledge, he replies it is from developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: Some mendicants ask Anuruddha how he remains equanimous even though ill. He replies that it is due to the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 3. Ethics and Duration
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks Anuruddha what makes someone a trainee. It is someone who has partly developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta and Moggallāna ask Anuruddha what a trainee mendicant should practice. He replies that it is the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In the Woods
suttas.blurb: A former partner of Venerable Anuruddha, now a deity named Jālinī, tries to tempt him with heavenly pleasures. But he has seen a higher happiness.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: When some mendicants ask Anuruddha how he attained such higher knowledge, he replies it is from developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 13. Kusinārā
suttas.blurb: Anuruddha complains of not being awakened.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks why Anuruddha looks so peaceful, and he replies it is due to developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: While meditating alone, Anuruddha reflected that one who has neglected the four kinds of mindfulness meditation has neglected the path to the end of suffering. Moggallāna reads his mind and visits to question him on this. Anuruddha speaks of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation internally and externally.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: Even if kings beg them to disrobe, a mendicant who has developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation is unmoved. Their mind flows to Nibbāna like the Ganges to the sea.

suttas.title: 5. Sabbath
suttas.blurb: Some “Agreeable” gods put on a display of music and dance for Venerable Anuruddha, but he is not impressed. He then asks the Buddha how women are reborn in such a heaven.

suttas.title: 1. In Private
suttas.blurb: While meditating alone, Anuruddha reflected that one who has neglected the four kinds of mindfulness meditation has neglected the path to the end of suffering. Moggallāna reads his mind and visits to question him on this. Anuruddha speaks of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation internally and externally, in terms of origin and cessation, and developing power over perceptions.

suttas.title: 3. Householders
suttas.blurb: Venerable Anuruddha reflects that the teachings is for someone who is of few wishes, content, secluded, energetic, mindful, peaceful, and wise. To these seven thoughts, the Buddha adds an eighth: the teaching is for those who delight in non-proliferation. Practicing in line with these eight thoughts of a great man, Venerable Anuruddha achieves perfection.

suttas.title: With Anuruddha
suttas.blurb: A lay person becomes confused when encouraged to develop the “limitless” and “expansive” liberations, and asks Venerable Anuruddha to explain whether they are the same or different.

suttas.blurb: Nothing is as effective for getting rid of fetters as the awakening factors.

suttas.title: 6. Discussion
suttas.blurb: Which awakening factors should be developed when the mind is tired, and which when it is energetic? And what is always useful?

name: Spirit & Towns (Protection)
description: 18 suttas on spirit, towns, protection, spirits (SN dominant)

suttas.title: At Ajakalāpaka
suttas.blurb: An awakened person has gone beyond fear.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A spirit was full of faith in the nun Sukkā, so he went about the towns and villages singing the benefits of giving her offerings.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A spirit was full of faith in the nun Cirā, so he went about the towns and villages singing the benefits of giving her offerings.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A female spirit hushes her son Piyaṅkara so she can hear the teaching and pursue harmlessness.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A spirit tells the Buddha that if he is truly free he should not be instructing others.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A spirit was full of faith in the nun Sukkā, so he went about the towns and villages encouraging people to listen to her teaching.

suttas.title: With Spiky
suttas.blurb: Another yakkha challenges the Buddha with riddles and threatens to “hurl out his mind, rip open his heart, or hurl him across the River Ganges” if he doesn’t solve the riddles to the yakkha’s satisfaction.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A local spirit asks the Buddha how a body is produced if there is no soul.

suttas.title: With Suppabuddha the Leper
suttas.blurb: A leper becomes a stream-enterer, dies, and is reborn as a deva.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: The spirits Khara and Sūciloma argue about whether the Buddha is a true ascetic or not. They approach him initially with threats, but then ask as to the origin of greed and hate.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: A female spirit hushes her children as she listens to the teaching. But her son Punabbasu says that he had not been talking, as he too wants to learn.

suttas.title: Suppavāsā
suttas.blurb: After a long and difficult pregnancy, Suppavāsā invites the Buddha and the Sangha for a meal.

suttas.title: With Āḷavaka
suttas.blurb: A yakkha challenges the Buddha with riddles and threatens to “hurl out his mind, rip open his heart, or hurl him across the River Ganges” if he doesn’t solve the riddles to the yakkha’s satisfaction.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: It seems that the novice Sānu had returned to his home intending to disrobe. To prevent him, a spirit possessed him, encouraging him to pursue the path of good. Sānu’s mother, weeping for her son, also tries to make him stay in robes.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: When Anāthapiṇḍika heard that a Buddha had arisen in the world, he rose first thing in the morning to go and visit him. But a mysterious darkness caused him to hesitate, so a spirit repeatedly encouraged him to step forward. Finally he reached the Buddha, and asked if he had slept well.

suttas.title: With Sabhiya
suttas.blurb: A sutta dating from early in the Buddha’s teaching career. A wanderer, disappointed in the teachings he has received from other teachers, approaches the Buddha with his questions.

suttas.title: 1. With Indaka
suttas.blurb: The famous story of the spirit Āḷavaka, who tries to bully the Buddha, but is soon persuaded through a surprisingly insightful series of questions and answers. This discourse provided the background for several elaborate legends in the later traditions

suttas.title: The Āṭānāṭiya Protection
suttas.blurb: Mighty spirits hold a congregation, and warn the Buddha that, since not all spirits are friendly, the mendicants should learn verses of protection.

name: Anāthapiṇḍika & Deity (Sarakāni)
description: 17 suttas on anāthapiṇḍika, deity, sarakāni, sāriputta (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: The deity Vasudatta says that a mendicant should live like their hair is on fire so as to give up lust. The Buddha changes his verse to emphasize giving up attachment to self.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: The deity Veṇhu celebrates the joy of those who follow the Buddha’s teaching. The Buddha affirms that such will escape death. Veṇhu is the Pali spelling of Sanskrit Viṣṇu, and this marks a rare appearance of a god of this name in the early texts.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: The deity Candimasa says that those who practice absorption will surely find escape. The Buddha adds that they must also be diligent and discard flaws.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: The god Uttara speaks of the transience of life, saying one should do good to be reborn in a happy future life. The Buddha replies that one should let go attachment.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: Subrahmā the god asks for the escape from fear. The Buddha says there is no escape from fear without letting go of everything.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: Candana asks who crosses the flood. The Buddha replies that it is one who is virtuous , wise, peaceful, and beyond attachment to rebirth.

suttas.title: 5. On Fire
suttas.blurb: Verses in praise of the Jetavana monastery, the Dhamma, and venerable Sāriputta, apparently spoken by the deity Anāthapiṇḍika.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: Dīghalaṭṭhi the god comes to the Buddha at Rājagaha and praises a mendicant who is a meditator.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: The deity Nandana asks the Buddha who is virtuous, wise, beyond suffering, worshiped by the gods themselves. The Buddha replies that such a person is an arahant.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: At Sāketa the god Kakudha asks the Buddha if he delights or sorrows. The Buddha replies that since he has no attachments he neither delights nor sorrows.

suttas.title: 2. With Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: A deity who had been the Buddha’s supporter Anāthapiṇḍika in his former life comes to Buddha and speaks verses in celebration of the Jeta’s Grove monastery—which he had offered for the Buddha’s use—good deeds, the Dhamma, and Venerable Sāriputta.

suttas.title: 18. A Lay Follower
suttas.blurb: The Buddha encourages Anāthapiṇḍika to not rest short with generosity, but to practice meditation too.

suttas.title: 6. The Undeclared Points
suttas.blurb: Anāthapiṇḍika’s home was in an uproar due to the badly behaved Sujātā, newly wedded to one of his sons. The Buddha speaks to her on the different kinds of wives.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: When the householder Anāthapiṇḍika was sick, Ānanda reminds him of the four factors of stream-entry, which Anāthapiṇḍika claims to possess.

suttas.title: 3. About Sarakāni
suttas.blurb: When the householder Anāthapiṇḍika was sick, Sāriputta reminds him of his considerable accomplishments in the Dhamma, analyzing stream-entry in ten factors, at which Anāthapiṇḍika recovers. Later he invited Sāriputta and Ānanda for a meal, and Sāriputta thanked him with verses.

suttas.title: 10. With Upāli
suttas.blurb: The Buddha teaches Anāthapiṇḍika ten kinds of persons who enjoy sensual pleasures.

suttas.title: Advice to Anāthapiṇḍika
suttas.blurb: As the great lay disciple Anāthapiṇḍika lies dying, Venerable Sāriputta visits him and gives a powerful teaching on non-attachment.

suttas.blurb: Like a stick falling on this side or that, beings are reborn in different realms.
