suttas.blurb: Transmigration is endless, like the grass and twigs in India.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: The bones you’ve left behind in transmigration are greater than a mountain.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: The tears you have shed in transmigration are greater than the waters in the oceans.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: Transmigration has no knowable beginning; even the oceans, mountains, and this great earth will perish. But like a dog on a leash running around a post, beings remain attached to the aggregates.

suttas.title: 10. Flowers
suttas.blurb: A dog leashed to a post will always remain close to that post. In the same way, beings remain close to aggregates in this endless cycle of transmigration. The Buddha refers to a well-known painting called “Conduct”, and says the mind is even more diverse than that; and indeed, the animal realm is the most diverse of them all, and it is produced by the mind.

name: Woods & Woods Woods (Deity)

suttas.title: 1. In the Woods

suttas.title: 1. In the Woods

suttas.title: 1. In the Woods

suttas.title: 1. In the Woods
suttas.blurb: When a mendicant falls asleep in the middle of the day, a deity tries to rouse them up. But not all is at it seems.

suttas.title: 4. Deities

suttas.blurb: Three ascetic perceptions lead to seven results.

suttas.title: 11. Perceptions for Ascetics
suttas.blurb: Ignorance is the precursor of wrong view and the other bad qualities, while right view is the opposite.

suttas.title: The Chapter on the Peaceful Mind
The Root of All Things
suttas.blurb: The Buddha examines how the notion of a permanent self emerges from the process of perception. A wide range of phenomena are considered, embracing both naturalistic and cosmological dimensions. An unawakened person interprets experience in terms of a self, while those more advanced have the same experiences without attachment.

suttas.blurb: The Buddha asks Ānanda if he likes Sāriputta, and when he replies that he does, the god Susīma and his assembly appear, rejoicing in Sāriputta’s praises, and radiating in lustrous colors.

suttas.title: 5. Old Age
suttas.blurb: The Buddha asks Sāriputta whether a noble disciple doubts the Buddha. Not only does he have faith, replies Sāriputta, but the other faculties each come to be developed in turn.

suttas.title: 2. At Nāḷandā
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta boldly claims that no-one has ever, or will ever, be more enlightened than the Buddha. He admits that he cannot read the minds of all the Buddhas, but he understands a principle of the Dhamma: all those who become awakened do so by giving up the five hindrances, practicing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, and developing the seven awakening factors.

suttas.title: 3. Individuals
suttas.blurb: Of various individuals on the path, which is best?

suttas.title: 2. At Nāḷandā
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta dies of illness, and the novice Cunda together with Ānanda take his bowl and robes and report the sad news to the Buddha. The Buddha dispels Ānanda’s sadness by speaking of the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: Inspiring Confidence
suttas.blurb: Shortly before he passes away, Venerable Sāriputta visits the Buddha and utters a moving eulogy of his great teacher.

suttas.title: Right View
suttas.blurb: Venerable Sāriputta gives a detailed explanation of right view, the first factor of the noble eightfold path. At the prompting of the other mendicants, he approaches the topic from a wide range of perspectives.

name: Cariyāpiṭaka: Yudhañjaya Chapter Perfections

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: While walking for alms down Vulture’s Peak, Venerable Moggallāna smiled at something invisible. Later, he told the Buddha it was a skeleton flying through the air, being pecked at by vultures. The Buddha confirmed that the man he had seen had been a butcher in his past life

suttas.title: Corruptions
suttas.blurb: A second discourse set at the quarrel of Kosambi, this depicts the Buddha, having failed to achieve reconciliation between the disputing mendicants, leaving the monastery. He spends time in the wilderness before encountering an inspiring community of practicing monks. There he discusses in detail obstacles to meditation that he encountered before awakening.

name: Forest & Cliff (Noble Truths)
description: 15 suttas on forest, cliff, noble truths, truths (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: As the dawn precedes the sunrise, right view precedes the penetration of the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: Beings who have not seen the four noble truths roam on from one birth to another, like a stick thrown end to end.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: Even more than if your clothes are on fire, you should make an effort to understand the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: Even more than if you’re being tortured with spikes, you should make an effort to understand the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: The small creatures in the ocean are greater in number than all the foliage in the jungles of India. Still, your rebirths are more than this.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: As long as there are no sun and moon, the world is in darkness. So too, as long as the Realized One has not arisen and declared the four noble truths, the world is in darkness.

suttas.title: 5. A Cliff
suttas.blurb: Just as it’s impossible to climb the upper floor without building the lower floors first, it’s impossible to end suffering without penetrating the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: In a siṁsapā grove, the Buddha holds up a handful of leaves, and compares it to all the leaves in the forest. So too, he has taught the four noble truths, which is only a fraction of what he knows.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: Just as it’s impossible to keep water in a basket of pine needles, it’s impossible to end suffering without penetrating the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 5. A Cliff
suttas.blurb: Interstellar space is dark and empty; but not seeing the four noble truths is even worse.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: No-one can refute you if you are well grounded in the four noble truths.

suttas.title: 5. A Cliff
suttas.blurb: The Buddha speaks of a hell called the great inferno, and points out that those who do not understand the four noble truths fall into a still fiercer inferno.

suttas.title: 5. A Cliff
suttas.blurb: The Buddha takes the mendicants to a steep precipice, and points out that those who do not understand the four noble truths fall into a still deeper precipice.

suttas.title: 4. In a Rosewood Forest
suttas.blurb: One who has not seen the four noble truths is as feeble as a tuft of cotton wool, while one who has seen them is like a deep-rooted pillar.

suttas.title: 5. A Cliff
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells the curious tale of a man who sat by a lotus pond to speculate about the world, and saw an army enter a lotus stalk. He thought he was going mad. The Buddha said that no, the army was real. Nevertheless, you should not speculate about such things, but should reflect on the four noble truths.

suttas.blurb: When the householder Mānadinna is sick, Ānanda visits him and encourages him to practice mindfulness meditation. But he has already developed them, and abandoned the five lower fetters.

suttas.title: Chapter One
suttas.blurb: Pañcālacaṇḍa praises the Buddha for finding the opening amid the confinement of the world. But the Buddha affirms that anyone with mindfulness and stillness may find such an escape.

suttas.title: 4. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Ānanda exclaims how amazing it is that the Buddha has found a way to freedom while still experiencing the world. Questioned by the monk Udāyī, Ānanda recounts a discussion on advanced meditation with the nun Jaṭilagāhiyā.

suttas.title: 8. Ānanda
suttas.blurb: Ānanda gets the Buddha to talk about the scale of the universe.

suttas.title: 2. Shaking the Stilt Longhouse
suttas.blurb: Ānanda explains to the brahmin Uṇṇābha that the spiritual path is lived to give up desire through the the four bases of psychic power. But Uṇṇābha says this is a contradiction, as desire is itself one of the four bases. Ānanda resolves the contradiction with the simile of a man walking to a park.

suttas.title: 10. With Upāli
suttas.blurb: The wanderer Kokanada meets Ānanda at the baths and engages him on the topic of the ten metaphysical questions.

suttas.title: 16. Faculties
suttas.blurb: A nun fakes illness as a pretext to seduce Ānanda.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta asks Ānanda how many things are given up and how many things possessed by the stream-enterer.

suttas.title: Advice to Puṇṇa
suttas.blurb: On the eve of his departure to a distant country, full of wild and unpredictable people, Venerable Puṇṇa is asked by the Buddha how he would respond if attacked there.

suttas.title: 6. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: The Buddha consoles Venerable Phagguṇa on his deathbed, and he dies at peace. The Buddha then explains to Ānanda the benefits of hearing the Dhamma at the right time.

suttas.title: The Wealthy Citizen
suttas.blurb: Asked by a householder to teach a path to freedom, Venerable Ānanda explains no less than eleven meditative states that may serve as doors to the deathless.

suttas.title: 2. Recollection
suttas.blurb: Asked by a householder to teach a path to freedom, Venerable Ānanda explains no less than eleven meditative states that may serve as doors to the deathless.

suttas.title: Advice to Channa
suttas.blurb: The monk Channa is suffering a painful terminal illness and wishes to take his own life.

suttas.title: With Subha
suttas.blurb: Shortly after the Buddha’s death, Venerable Ānanda is invited to explain the core teachings.

name: Nuns & Māra (Asks Nun)
description: 14 suttas on nuns, māra, asks nun, rejects (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra asks the nun Cālā why she disapproves of rebith. She replies that it necessarily entails suffering.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra asks the nun Sīsupacālā whose teaching she believes in. She replies that she rejects the various sectarians and follows the Buddha, as his teachings lead to freedom.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra asks the nun Upacālā where she wishes to be reborn. But as the world everywhere is burning, she rejects all forms of rebirth.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra asks the nun Selā about who has created this “puppet”, i.e. the form that has been reincarnated. She replies that there is no creator, but it arises as a result of conditions, like the growing of a seed in a field.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra comes to the nun Vijayā in the form of a handsome youth and tries to tempt her from her path. But she rejects sensual pleasures, as she delights in subtle meditation and has ended ignorance.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: When meditating in the Dark Wood, the nun Somā is taunted by Māra, who claims that a mere woman can never see the subtle truth. She rejects him, stating that gender does not matter when one is perceiving deep truths, and telling Māra to go see those still attached to gender.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Uppalavaṇṇā is rebuked by Māra, who says a beautiful woman like her should fear rogues when alone in the forest. However, she dismisses such fears, saying she can use her psychic powers to defeat thousands of rogues like him.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: The nun Āḷavikā has entered the Dark Wood seeking seclusion when Māra comes to threaten her, claiming there is no escape and no point in seclusion. She recognizes him instantly and dismisses his taunts, asserting that she is beyond his control.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra taunts the nun Kisāgotamī, alleging she is mourning sons and longing for a man. She rejects him, stating that she has no need for men, as she has destroyed the mass of darkness and conquered the army of Death.

suttas.title: 1. Nuns
suttas.blurb: Māra asks the nun Vajirā about who has created this being. Recognizing him, she points out that the word “being” is nothing more than a convention used to designate the aggregates, just as the word “cart” is used when the parts are assembled

suttas.blurb: Teachers and audience should have understanding.

suttas.blurb: A list of ten doctrinal factors

suttas.title: 11. Awakening
suttas.blurb: Indulgences never satisfy.

suttas.title: 1. Awakening
suttas.blurb: Nine kinds of persons: the eight noble persons and the ordinary person.

suttas.title: 1. Awakening
suttas.blurb: The things that a mendicant relies on as supports in spiritual practice.

suttas.title: 6. Involvement
suttas.blurb: Consciousness is like a seed that is planted in the soil of the other four aggregates and watered with craving.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: Liberation doesn’t happen because you wish for it, but because you develop the path.

suttas.title: 1. Awakening
suttas.blurb: Four kinds of person who one should or should not associate with. A similar distinction is made regarding robes, almsfood, lodgings, towns, and countries.

name: Praise & Inspired (Speak)
description: 13 suttas on praise, inspired, speak, verses (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Remembering his past as a wandering poet, Vaṅgīsa celebrates the freedom he has found in the Dhamma

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Troubled once more by lust, Vaṅgīsa turned to Ānanda for support. Ānanda tells him to shift his perception so that beautiful things no longer seem so.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: As a renowned poet, Vaṅgīsa looked down on less skilled mendicants. Ashamed of his conceit, he exhorted himself in verse to be humble and at peace.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Vaṅgīsa says the Buddha, like the moon or the sun, outshines the whole world.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Vaṅgīsa speaks in praise of Moggallāna and the Saṅgha.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Seeing Sāriputta speak on the Dhamma, and the rapt attention of the Saṅgha, Vaṅgīsa comes out with verses in his praise.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Aññā Koṇḍañña, the very first disciple to realize the Dhamma, comes to visit the Buddha after long in seclusion. Inspired, Vaṅgīsa speaks verses in his praise.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Enticed by the sight of beautiful women, the newly ordained monk Vaṅgīsa dispels desire and celebrates his commitment to the spiritual path in verse.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Vaṅgīsa is troubled again by lust, but manages to dispel it through his own efforts. His verse speak of the way of the renunciate, letting go of desire and wrong views, and dedicated to ethical conduct and seclusion.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: The Buddha gives a simple teaching on four qualities of good speech. Vaṅgīsa is inspired to utter a more elaborate set of verses on the topic, praising especially the speech of the Buddha that leads to the Deathless.

suttas.title: Vaṅgīsa and His Mentor Nigrodhakappa
suttas.blurb: Vaṅgīsa, the foremost poet among the Buddha’s disciples, praises the Buddha in verse.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: Seeing the Buddha speak on the Dhamma, and the rapt attention of the Saṅgha, Vaṅgīsa comes out with verses in his praise. When asked if the verses were spontaneous, he replies that they were, and the Buddha invites him to speak further.

suttas.title: 1. With Vaṅgīsa
suttas.blurb: On the Pavāraṇā day at the end of the rains retreat, the Buddha invites the Saṅgha to admonish him. But not only is the Buddha faultless, so are all the 500 monks present. Vaṅgīsa is inspired to utter a elaborate praise of the Buddha.

name: Chief & Asks (Rebirth)
description: 13 suttas on chief, asks, rebirth, head (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Assāroha the cavalry warrior asks the Buddha whether the belief that warriors have a good rebirth is correct. The Buddha tries to dissuade him, but ultimately reveals that by following the way of violence they head to a bad rebirth. Yodhājı̄va is distressed and asks to ordain.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Hatthāroha the elephant warrior asks the Buddha whether the belief that warriors have a good rebirth is correct. The Buddha tries to dissuade him, but ultimately reveals that by following the way of violence they head to a bad rebirth. Hatthāroha is distressed and takes refuge.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: A headman known for his aggressive nature approaches the Buddha and asks how to give up irritability. The Buddha points out that such reactions are fueled by greed, hate, and delusion.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Several members of the king’s retinue criticize the Buddhist mendicants for accepting offerings of money. Chief Maṇicūḷaka is among them, and he visits the Buddha to find out the truth. The Buddha categorically denies the accusation.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Yodhājı̄va the professional warrior asks the Buddha whether the belief that warriors have a good rebirth is correct. The Buddha tries to dissuade him, but ultimately reveals that by following the way of violence they head to a bad rebirth. Yodhājı̄va is distressed and takes refuge.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Mahāvīra asks Asibandhakaputta to refute the Buddha on behalf of the Jains. He suggests to try to trap the Buddha with a dilemma: he claims to have compassion for householders, yet visits them with a large Saṅgha in a time of scarcity. But the Buddha says no family has been harmed from this.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: Talapuṭa the head of a troupe of performers asks the Buddha whether the belief that performers have a good rebirth is correct. The Buddha tries to dissuade him, but ultimately reveals that by inciting lust they head to a bad rebirth. Talapuṭa is distressed and asks to ordain.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The chief Bhadraka asks the Buddha about the origin and ending of suffering, and the Buddha points out that a loss among Bhadraka’s own community would cause him suffering, due to his attachment.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The chief Asibandhakaputta asks whether the rituals advocated by the western brahmins can bring a person to heaven. The Buddha asserts that only good deeds can take you to a good rebirth, and rituals have no effect.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The chief Asibandhakaputta asks the Buddha why, if he has equal compassion for all, he teaches some more than others. The Buddha answers with a simile of a field: a farmer knows to put most of their effort into the fertile land.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The Buddha asks the chief Asibandhakaputta, a Jain, how Mahāvīra the Jain leader teaches his followers. He replies that you go to a rebirth depending on what you usually do. But the Buddha points out that even a killer only spends a small amount of time actually killing. The Buddha’s approach is to point out that deeds have results, and to encourage the abandoing of bad deeds and the adoption of good deeds.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The chief Rāsiya asks whether the Buddha completely rejects all forms of asceticism. The Buddha denies that this is the case, and explains what he means by the middle way in a lengthy disquisition, covering useful and useless forms of asceticism.

suttas.title: 1. Chiefs
suttas.blurb: The chief Pāṭaliya asks whether the Buddha knows magic. He says he does—but that does not make him a magician.

name: Meditation & Mindfulness (Mindfulness Meditation)
description: 13 suttas on meditation, mindfulness, mindfulness meditation, fulfill (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 16. The End of Relishing
suttas.blurb: A mendicant should make an effort in seclusion in order to truly understand the origin and ending of the process of sense experience.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Answering some mendicants, the Buddha explains how one thing fulfills four things, four things fulfill seven things, and seven things fulfill two things.

suttas.title: 2. Meditation
suttas.blurb: The Buddha goes on retreat for a fortnight. When he emerges, he describes his meditation on the conditionality of the eightfold path, which was similar to a practice he did shortly after awakening.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to some mendicants how one thing fulfills four things, four things fulfill seven things, and seven things fulfill two things.

suttas.title: 2. Meditation
suttas.blurb: The Buddha goes on retreat for a fortnight. When he emerges, he describes his meditation on the conditionality of the eightfold path, which was similar to a practice he did shortly after awakening.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: The Buddha goes on a three month retreat, and later says that during that time he mostly practiced breath meditation.

suttas.title: How to Stop Thinking
suttas.blurb: In a practical meditation teaching, the Buddha describes five different approaches to stopping thoughts.

suttas.title: Self-Effacement
suttas.blurb: The Buddha differentiates between peaceful meditation and spiritual practices that encompass the whole of life. He lists forty-four aspects, which he explains as “effacement”, the wearing away of conceit.

suttas.title: Mindfulness of Breathing
suttas.blurb: Surrounded by many well-practiced mendicants, the Buddha teaches mindfulness of breathing in detail, showing how it relates to the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: Mindfulness Meditation
suttas.blurb: Here the Buddha details the seventh factor of the noble eightfold path, mindfulness meditation. This collects many of the meditation teachings found throughout the canon, especially the foundational practices focusing on the body, and is regarded as one of the most important meditation discourses.

suttas.title: Mindfulness of the Body
suttas.blurb: This focuses on the first aspect of mindfulness meditation, the observation of the body. This set of practices, simple as they seem, have far-reaching benefits.

suttas.title: The Great Forty
suttas.blurb: A discourse on the prerequisites of right samādhi that emphasizes the interrelationship and mutual support of all the factors of the eightfold path.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse on Mindfulness Meditation
suttas.blurb: The Buddha details the seventh factor of the noble eightfold path, mindfulness meditation. This discourse is essentially identical to MN 10, with the addition of an extended section on the four noble truths derived from MN 141.

name: Naḷakapāna & Attendant (Speaks)
description: 13 suttas on naḷakapāna, attendant, speaks, 12 kesi (AN dominant)

suttas.title: A Fork in the Road
suttas.blurb: One of the Buddha’s attendants disobeys him.

suttas.title: 12. With Kesi
suttas.blurb: Four inspiring places to visit.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: Brahmā Sanaṅkumāra appears to the Buddha and asserts that the aristocrat or khattiya is the best caste, but the Buddha is the best of people.

suttas.title: 3. With Five Factors
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells Venerable Nāgita how he dislikes noisy crowds, and wishes never to become famous.

suttas.title: 4. Deities
suttas.blurb: When visited by a noisy crowd, the Buddha speaks to his attendant Nāgita of his love of seclusion and solitude, and of his regard for forest mendicants.

suttas.title: 9. Mindfulness
suttas.blurb: When the householders of Icchānaṅgala make a racket in the monastery, the Buddha asks his attendant Nāgita what is going on. The Buddha speaks strongly of his dislike for material gains, and his love of seclusion.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: The high and luxurious beds that the Buddha uses.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: How to navigate among different spiritual opinions.

suttas.title: With Sela
suttas.blurb: Sela the brahman praises the Buddha to see how the latter responds to praise.

suttas.title: With Sela
suttas.blurb: A brahmanical ascetic named Keṇiya invites the entire Saṅgha for a meal. When the brahmin Sela sees what is happening, he visits the Buddha and expresses his delight in a moving series of devotional verses.

suttas.title: At Naḷakapāna
suttas.blurb: Those who practice do so not because they are failures, but because they aspire to higher freedom. When he speaks of the attainments of disciples, the Buddha does so in order to inspire.

suttas.title: With Raṭṭhapāla
suttas.blurb: A wealthy young man, Raṭṭhapāla, has a strong aspiration to go forth, but has to prevail against the reluctance of his parents. Even after he became a monk, his parents tried to persuade him to disrobe. The discourse ends with a moving series of teachings on the fragility of the world.

suttas.title: With Mahāli
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to a diverse group of lay people how the results of meditation depend on the manner of development.

name: Iron & Ball (Psychic)
description: 12 suttas on iron, ball, psychic, psychic power (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The four bases for psychic power are desire, energy, higher mind, and inquiry.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The four bases for psychic power lead to perfection or once-return.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: It is because of developing the four bases of psychic power that a mendicant ends the defilements.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The four bases for psychic power lead to seven benefits; perfection or one of the lesser attainments.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to several mendicants the difference between psychic powers, the basis for psychic power, and the development of the bases for psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to Ānanda the difference between psychic powers, the basis for psychic power, and the development of the bases for psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains to several mendicants the difference between psychic powers, the basis for psychic power, and the development of the bases for psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: Before his awakening, the Buddha reflected on the path for developing the bases of psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna is so very powerful due to developing the four bases for psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: The Realized One is so very powerful due to developing the four bases for psychic power.

suttas.title: 3. The Iron Ball
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha whether he travels to the Brahmā realm in a mind-made body or in a physical body.

name: Fears & 13 Fears (Fears 13)
description: 12 suttas on fears, 13 fears, fears 13, royal monastery (AN dominant)

suttas.title: 8. Slain
suttas.blurb: Why is it that, though the path has been taught, people still fear?

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple has transcended fears of a bad rebirth.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Monks, nuns, laywomen, and laymen love to hear Dharma from Ānanda.

suttas.title: 2. The Royal Monastery
suttas.blurb: A noble disciple has transcended fears of a bad rebirth.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Contemplating jhānas with wisdom.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Even in a world full of attachment, people listen to the Buddha’s teaching.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Contemplating divine abiding meditations with wisdom.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: The Buddha illuminates even interstellar space, so that beings reborn there see each other.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Rebirth in Brahmā realms from divine abiding meditations.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: The fears of guilt, shame, punishment, and rebirth in a bad place.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: Length of rebirth in various exalted realms.

suttas.title: 13. Fears
suttas.blurb: The risks of waves, crocodiles, whirlpools, and sharks.

name: Powers Trainee & Trainee (Powers)
description: 12 suttas on powers trainee, trainee, powers, trainee powers (AN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: Loss of foundational qualities leads to decline.

suttas.title: 16. The True Teaching
suttas.blurb: Confidence or lack thereof in mendicants is due to their practice.

suttas.title: 3. Householders
suttas.blurb: Eight very different kinds of powers.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: Five qualities determine rebirth.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: Five powers of a trainee.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: A mendicant with five bad qualities fails in the Dharma.

suttas.title: 11. Living Comfortably
suttas.blurb: Five factors in which a trainee is assured.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: A disrespectful mendicant with five bad qualities fails in the Dharma.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: A mendicant with five bad qualities can’t grow in the Dharma.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: Reasons to criticize a mendicant who disrobes, or praise one who remains.

suttas.title: 1. Powers of a Trainee
suttas.blurb: Suffering in this life and the next.

name: Mango Grove & Mango (Grove)
description: 12 suttas on mango grove, mango, grove, mindfulness meditation (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: At Ambapālī’s mango grove, the Buddha teaches the mendicants about the four kinds of mindfulness meditation and the practice of situational awareness.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: The four kinds of mindfulness meditation are a complete heap of what is skillful.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: At Ambapālī’s mango grove, the Buddha teaches the mendicants about the “way to convergence”, the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 2. At Nāḷandā
suttas.blurb: Just after the Buddha’s awakening, Brahmā Sahampati supports the Buddha’s reflection that the four kinds of mindfulness meditation are the way to convergence.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: The parable of the quail and the hawk. When the quail ventured outside her ancestral territory, she became vulnerable. And what is a mendicant’s ancestral territory? The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: The parable of the foolish monkey who gets trapped in tar when it ventures outside its ancestral territory. And what is a mendicant’s ancestral territory? The four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: Newly ordained mendicants should practice the four kinds of mindfulness meditation; trainees are practicing them, and perfected ones have perfected the practice.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: When a mendicant asks for a teaching to take on retreat, the Buddha issues an unexpected rebuke. Evidently the mendicant had been badly behaved; but now, he insists, he is sincere. The Buddha relents, and teaches the four kinds of mindfulness meditation, well grounded on ethics.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: The parable of the cook. They present different kinds of dishes to the king, and observe which the king likes. In the same way, a mendicant observes why their meditation succeeds or fails. When the four kinds of mindfulness meditation succeeds it leads to giving up hindrances and entering immersion.

suttas.title: Jungle Thickets
suttas.blurb: While living in the wilderness is great, not everyone is ready for it. The Buddha encourages meditators to reflect on whether one’s environment is genuinely supporting their meditation practice, and if not, to leave.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: The Buddha decides to spend his final rains retreat at Vesālī in Beluvagāmaka. During the retreat he becomes very ill, but later recovers. Ānanda wonders who will guide the Saṅgha when the Buddha dies, but the Buddha says they should be their own refuge, grounded on the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.

suttas.title: 1. In Ambapālī’s Mango Grove
suttas.blurb: When Ānanda visits the nuns’s quarters they tell him that their meditation is prospering to higher and higher levels. Ānanda reporrts the good news to the Buddha, who speaks of two ways of developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation: directed and undirected.

name: Wealth & Wealth Wealth (Views Doubt)
description: 12 suttas on wealth, wealth wealth, views doubt, doubt conceit (AN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, envy, and stinginess.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The powers of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, samādhi, and wisdom.

suttas.title: A Treasure Trove
suttas.blurb: Merit is the only sure investment

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The wealth of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The purpose of the spiritual life is to give up the fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.

suttas.title: 26. Insight
suttas.blurb: Why families lose wealth.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: Seven qualities that make a mendicant either agreeable or disagreeable to their fellow monastics.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: Migāra of Rohaṇa is a wealthy man, but even his riches are vulnerable to the vicissitudes of life, unlike spiritual wealth.

suttas.title: 5. With King Muṇḍa
suttas.blurb: Kinds of spiritual wealth, in detail.

suttas.title: The Wealthy Rancher
suttas.blurb: A poetic dialogue contrasting the wealth and security of lay life with the wealth and security of a person who has lived the renunciate life to its culmination. If you have trouble relating to someone like Dhaniya who measures his wealth in cattle, then when reading this poem substitute stocks and bonds for cows and bulls, and economic downturn for rain.

suttas.title: 1. Wealth
suttas.blurb: The wealth of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom.

name: Brahmā & Brahmās (Sahampati)
description: 12 suttas on brahmā, brahmās, sahampati, brahmā sahampati (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: The brahmās Subrahmā and Suddhāvāsa come to the Buddha’s hut and speak on the monk Katamorakatissaka, a follower of Devadatta.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: The brahmās Subrahmā and Suddhāvāsa come to the Buddha’s hut and speak on the monk Kokālika, a follower of Devadatta.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: The brahmā Tudu tries to persuade Kokālika to have faith in Sāriputta and Moggallāna, but Kokālika wants none of it.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: The brahmās Subrahmā and Suddhāvāsa note that a fellow-brahmā is living in negligence, overly impressed with his own glory, so they go to shake him from his complacence.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: An unnamed brahmā god thinks to himself that he is so powerful no asectic can approach him. The Buddha goes straight to his realm and appears wrapped in flame above his head. Mahāmoggallāna and several other disciples follow him. The brahmā and his retinue are sutiably impressed.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: Brahmā Sahampati appears before a woman who is a devotee of his, and tells her to offer food instead to her son, a Buddhist monk, as Brahmās don’t eat the food she offers.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: Baka the Brahmā conceives the wrong view that his status is permanent and imperishable. The Buddha goes to dissuade him, and they have an exchange in verse, with the Buddha telling Baka of his past lives.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: After his awakening, the Buddha reflects that one should live with reverence; but who is he to revere, since he is fully awakened? He realizes that he should live revering the Dhamma. Brahmā Sahampati appears and agrees with him, saying that this is how all Buddhas of past and future live too.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: Kokālika goes to the Buddha and criticizes Sāriputta and Moggallāna. But having deliberately slandered such great monks, he dies and Brahmā Sahampati informs the Buddha he has been reborn in hell. The Buddha gives a memorable simile of the length of life in hell.

suttas.title: With Kokālika
suttas.blurb: A follower of Devadatta slanders Ven. Sāriputta and Ven. Moggallāna and, after suffering a painful disease, falls into hell. The sutta then gives a graphic description of the sufferings awaiting him there.

suttas.title: 1. The Appeal
suttas.blurb: After his awakening, the Buddha hesitated to teach, thinking that the Dhamma is too subtle for people to understand. But the high divinity Brahmā Sahampati appears and encourages him to teach, pointing out that there are some with “little dust in their eyes” who will understand.

suttas.title: 9. Senior Mendicants
suttas.blurb: The monk Kokālika accuses Sāriputta and Moggallāna of having evil wishes. The Buddha defends them. Later, Kokālika suffers calamity and is reborn in hell.

name: Perception & State (Normal)
description: 12 suttas on perception, state, normal, meditation normal (AN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the dimension of nothingness was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the dimension of infinite consciousness was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the dimension of infinite space was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

suttas.title: 1. By Moggallāna
suttas.blurb: Moggallāna speaks to the mendicants of a time when his practice of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception was faltering. He expresses his gratitude for the Buddha, who urged him to stabilize that attainment.

suttas.title: 1. Dependence
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about a deep state of meditation where all normal attention has ceased, but there is still attention. The Buddha affirms that such a state exists.

suttas.title: 2. Recollection
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is asked by the mendicants about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception.

suttas.title: 2. Recollection
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta is asked by the mendicants about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception.

suttas.title: 2. Recollection
suttas.blurb: Sāriputta teaches the mendicants about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception.

suttas.title: 2. Recollection
suttas.blurb: The Buddha teaches the mendicants about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception.

suttas.title: 1. Benefits
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about a mysterious state of immersion in meditation where there is no normal perception, yet one is still conscious.

suttas.title: 1. Dependence
suttas.blurb: Ānanda asks the Buddha about a deep state of meditation where all normal perception has ceased, but there is still perception. The Buddha affirms that such a state exists. Ānanda puts the same question to Sāriputta, and gets the same answer.

suttas.title: 1. Dependence
suttas.blurb: The Buddha tells Venerable Sandha to meditate like a trained thoroughbred, not like a wild colt. Doing so, they may attain a deep state.

name: Longer & Shorter (Emptiness)
description: 12 suttas on longer, shorter, emptiness, vacchagotta (MN dominant)

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse at Gosiṅga
suttas.blurb: Several senior mendicants, reveling in the beauty of the night, discuss what kind of practitioner would adorn the park. They take their answers to the Buddha, who praises their answers, but gives his own twist.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse at Gosiṅga
suttas.blurb: The Buddha comes across three mendicants practicing diligently and harmoniously, and asks them how they do it. Reluctant to disclose their higher attainments, they explain how they deal with the practical affairs of living together. But when pressed by the Buddha, they reveal their meditation attainments.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse With Māluṅkyaputta
suttas.blurb: A monk demands that the Buddha answer his metaphysical questions, or else he will disrobe. The Buddha compares him to a man struck by an arrow, who refuses treatment until he can have all his questions about the arrow and the archer answered.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse With Māluṅkya
suttas.blurb: A little baby has no wrong views or intentions, but the underlying tendency for these things is still there. Without practicing, they will inevitably recur.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse on the Full-Moon Night
suttas.blurb: On a lovely full moon night, one of the mendicants presents the Buddha with a series of questions that go to the heart of the teaching. But when he hears of the doctrine of not-self, another mendicant is unable to grasp the meaning.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse With Vacchagotta
suttas.blurb: In the final installment of the “Vacchagotta trilogy”, Vacchagotta lets go his obsession with meaningless speculation, and asks about practice.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse on Emptiness
suttas.blurb: The Buddha describes his own practice of the meditation on emptiness.

suttas.title: The Shorter Elaboration
suttas.blurb: The layman Visākha asks the nun Dhammadinnā about various difficult matters, including some of the highest meditation attainments. The Buddha fully endorses her answers.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse With Saccaka
suttas.blurb: Saccaka was a debater, who challenged the Buddha to a contest. Despite his bragging, the Buddha is not at all perturbed at his attacks.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse With Saccaka
suttas.blurb: In a less confrontational meeting, the Buddha and Saccaka discuss the difference between physical and mental development. The Buddha gives a long account of the various practices he did before awakening, detailing the astonishing lengths he took to mortify the body.

suttas.title: The Longer Discourse on Emptiness
suttas.blurb: A group of mendicants have taken to socializing too much, so the Buddha teaches on the importance of seclusion in order to enter fully into emptiness.

suttas.title: The Longer Analysis of Deeds
suttas.blurb: Confronted with an overly simplistic version of his own teachings, the Buddha emphasizes the often overlooked nuances and qualifications in how karma plays out.

name: Dhammika & Dhammika Dhammika (Subha)
description: 12 suttas on dhammika, dhammika dhammika, subha, quest (AN dominant)

suttas.title: With Soṇa
suttas.blurb: A young man in a remote part of India is able to ordain only after many delays.

suttas.title: 5. About Dhammika
suttas.blurb: Two monks separately come to the Buddha and speak of awakening in terms of letting go conceit. The Buddha affirms that they are, in fact, awakened, and that such indirect means are the proper way to make such declarations.

suttas.title: With Dhammika
suttas.blurb: The proper code of conduct for lay followers of the Dhamma.

suttas.title: 5. About Dhammika
suttas.blurb: When going for a bath, the Buddha encounters a giant royal elephant. But a spiritual giant is even more impressive.

suttas.title: 5. About Dhammika
suttas.blurb: In response to criticism, the Buddha explains that it is difficult to compare the spiritual development of different individuals, so we should not be judgmental.

suttas.title: 5. About Dhammika
suttas.blurb: The monk Dhammika annoys both the monks and the layfolk so badly that they banish him. He comes to the Buddha, who consoles him and raises his spirits with a story, while at the same time showing him the error of his ways.

suttas.title: At Madhurā
suttas.blurb: In Madhurā, towards the north-eastern limit of the Buddha’s reach during his life, King Avantiputta asks Venerable Mahākaccāna regarding the brahmanical claim to be the highest caste.

suttas.title: With Uggāhamāna Samaṇamaṇḍikāputta
suttas.blurb: A wanderer teaches that a person has reached the highest attainment when they keep four basic ethical precepts. The Buddha’s standards are considerably higher.

suttas.title: With Subha
suttas.blurb: Working hard is not valuable in and of itself; what matters is the outcome. And just as in lay life, spiritual practice may or may not lead to fruitful results.

suttas.title: The Noble Quest
suttas.blurb: This is one of the most important biographical discourses, telling the Buddha’s experiences from leaving home to realizing awakening. Throughout, he was driven by the imperative to fully escape from rebirth and suffering.

suttas.title: With Poṭṭhapāda
suttas.blurb: The Buddha discusses with a wanderer the nature of perception and how it evolves through deeper states of meditation. None of these, however, should be identified with a self or soul.

suttas.title: About Pāṭikaputta
suttas.blurb: When Sunakkhatta threatens to disrobe, the Buddha is unimpressed. Rejecting showy displays of asceticism or wondrous powers, he demonstrates his pre-eminence.

name: Lay Followers & Followers (Brahmin)
description: 12 suttas on lay followers, followers, brahmin, work (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains that a true mendicant is one who has gone beyond evil.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin wonders whether the legitimate seeking of alms is a proper way to support his parents.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin is enjoying his work in the woods. Seeing the Buddha in meditation, he wonders what sort of work the Buddha enjoys.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin complains when the Buddha visits for alms again and again. The Buddha takes the chance to point out that all natural phenomena repeat in cycles, and only an awakened one escapes the cycle.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: When the Buddha approaches a brahmin council in session, they complain that he is out of order.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: Some brahmin students are collecting firewood when they see the Buddha in meditation. They return and inform their teacher, who visits the Buddha to ask him how he is so peaceful in the wild forest.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is ill and sends his attendant monk Upavāṇa to fetch some hot water. A brahmin gives him not just water, but also molasses to ease his stomach. Later the brahmin visits the Buddha to discuss generosity.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin believes that by bathing he can wash away his sins. But the Buddha speaks of the Dhamma as a lake for true purification.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin in shabby clothes has been kicked out of his home by ungrateful sons. The Buddha gives him a verse to recite in the village council, comparing his faithless sons to his trusty wooden staff. Ashamed, the sons take back their father, bathe and dress him.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A stuck-up brahmin listening to the Buddha teach is awestruck when the Buddha appears to read his mind. To the surprise of the crowd, he bows and kisses the Buddha’s feet. The Buddha tells him to take his seat, and they exchange verses on the topic of conceit.

suttas.title: 2. Lay Followers
suttas.blurb: A brahmin farmer criticizes the Buddha for failing to produce food, merely living off the work of others. But the Buddha shows that his true work is spiritual.

suttas.title: 17. With Jānussoṇi
suttas.blurb: Cunda the smith tells the Buddha of rites of purification practiced by the western brahmins. The Buddha explains purification in his teaching.

name: Eleven-Factored Teachings (Ekadasaka Nipāta)
description: Abbreviated Aṅguttara Nikāya texts from the Book of Elevens (AN 11), presenting condensed teachings enumerated in groups of eleven factors.

name: Diverse & Elements Sense (Elements)
description: 11 suttas on diverse, elements sense, elements, rise (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: The senses function due to the various different external elements of sense experience, namely, the various sense stimuli.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: The diverse internal elements of sense experience give rise to diverse contacts.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: The senses function due to the various different elements of sense experience, namely, the internal sense organ, the external sense stimulus, and consciousness.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: It is not the case that diverse contacts give rise to the diverse elements of sense experience.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: Diverse contacts give rise to diverse feelings.

suttas.title: 13. Householders
suttas.blurb: The householder Ghosita asks Ānanda about the diversity of elements, and he answers in terms of the elements of sense experience.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: It is not the case that diverse feelings give rise to diverse contacts.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: It is not the case that diverse perceptions, intentions, desires, passions, and quests give rise to diverse external elements of sense experience.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: The diverse external elements of sense experience give rise to diverse perceptions, intentions, desires, passions, and quests.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: The diverse external elements of sense experience give rise to diverse perceptions, intentions, desires, passions, quests, and material possessions.

suttas.title: 1. Diversity
suttas.blurb: It is not the case that diverse perceptions, intentions, desires, passions, quests, and material possessions give rise to diverse external elements of sense experience.

suttas.title: The Discourse on Love
suttas.blurb: The practice of developing universal goodwill: the practices that form a foundation for the practice, the attitude of universal goodwill itself, and the steps that lead from goodwill to awakening.

suttas.title: Chapter Two

suttas.title: The Book of the Threes
suttas.blurb: Like attracts like. It’s in accordance with their properties—either low or admirable—that beings come together and associate with one another.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: People of poor character associate together, while people of good character associate together.

suttas.title: Chapter Two

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: People of poor character associate together, while people of good character associate together. Illustrated with similes and verses.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: Laziness is an obstruction to the path.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: Does anyone have a sense of conscience and shame?

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: The quiet murmuring of the jungle can be scary—but not to the Buddha.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: A god’s worldly values are improved by the Buddha’s version.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: We think our attachments bring us happiness, but they really bring sorrow.

suttas.title: Chapter Two
suttas.blurb: When Sakka asks what place is truly delightful, the Buddha replies that it is wherever the perfected ones dwell.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight
suttas.blurb: Life is hard for an unrestrained ascetic; they should draw in their thoughts like a tortoise.

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight

suttas.title: 2. The Garden of Delight

name: Migajāla & Senses (Migajāla Migajāla)
description: 11 suttas on migajāla, senses, migajāla migajāla, understand (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 14. At Devadaha
suttas.blurb: The Buddha speaks of the hell named the “six senses” where all one experiences is sheer pain and unpleasantness.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: One who does not understand the six senses’ origin, ending, gratification, drawback, and escape is no true mendicant. A mendicant speaks up, saying that he does not truly understand these things, so the Buddha teaches him further.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: One who does not understand the six senses’ origin, ending, gratification, drawback, and escape is no true mendicant. A mendicant speaks up, saying that he does not truly understand these things, so the Buddha teaches him further.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: One who does not understand the six senses’ origin, ending, gratification, drawback, and escape is no true mendicant. A mendicant speaks up, saying that he does not truly understand these things, so the Buddha teaches him further.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Migajāla asks for a teaching to take on retreat. The Buddha teaches him that the senses are delightful, but if you hold to them you will suffer. Meditating on this while on retreat, Migajāla became awakened.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: The teaching that can be realized in this very life is the desire for the senses, and letting go of that desire.

suttas.title: 7. With Migajāla
suttas.blurb: Venerable Migajāla asks how one lives alone, and how with a partner. The Buddha says that so long as one is bound by desire to the senses, one lives with a partner. A mendicant free of such desire dwells alone, even if they live in close association with worldly people.

suttas.title: The Great Discourse on What Relates to the Six Sense Fields
suttas.blurb: Explains how insight into the six senses is integrated with the eightfold path and leads to liberation.

suttas.title: The Sixfold Purification
suttas.blurb: If someone claims to be awakened, their claim should be interrogated with a detailed series of detailed questions. Only if they can answer them clearly should the claim be accepted.

suttas.title: Six By Six
suttas.blurb: The Buddha analyzes the six senses from six perspectives, and demonstrates the emptiness of all of them.

suttas.title: The Analysis of the Six Sense Fields
suttas.blurb: A detailed analysis of the six senses and the relation to emotional and cognitive processes.

name: Lesser & Conditioned (Difference)
description: 11 suttas on lesser, conditioned, difference (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 5. The Lesser Chapter
suttas.blurb: The difference between conditioned and unconditioned.

name: Elements & Taking (Practices)
description: 11 suttas on elements, taking, practices, cultivated (MN dominant)

suttas.title: 13. At Kusinārā
suttas.blurb: When mendicants fight, the Buddha doesn’t like it.

suttas.title: Quarrels and Disputes
suttas.blurb: The Buddha is questioned on the source of quarrels and disputes, and on the highest level of spiritual attainment.

suttas.title: 5. Abuse
suttas.blurb: Some monks are arguing, so the Buddha teaches them ten warm-hearted principles.

suttas.title: The Shorter Discourse on Taking Up Practices
suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains how taking up different practices may have harmful or beneficial results. The memorable simile of the creeper shows how insidious temptations can be.

suttas.title: The Great Discourse on Taking Up Practices
suttas.blurb: While we all want to be happy, we often find the opposite happens. The Buddha explains why.

suttas.title: The Mendicants of Kosambī
suttas.blurb: Despite the Buddha’s presence, the monks of Kosambi fell into a deep and bitter dispute. The Buddha taught the reluctant monks to develop love and harmony, reminding them of the state of peace that they sought.

suttas.title: 7. The Great Chapter
suttas.blurb: It would be better for a monk to embrace a blazing fire than to lie with a maiden.

suttas.title: Many Elements
suttas.blurb: Beginning by praising a wise person, the Buddha goes on to explain that one becomes wise by inquiring into the elements, sense fields, dependent origination, and what is possible and impossible.

suttas.title: The Analysis of No Strife
suttas.blurb: Achieving peace is no simple matter. The Buddha explains how to avoid conflict through contentment, right speech, understanding pleasure, and not insisting on local conventions.

suttas.title: What Should and Should Not Be Cultivated
suttas.blurb: The Buddha sets up a framework on things to be cultivated or avoided, and Venerable Sāriputta volunteers to elaborate.

suttas.title: The Analysis of the Elements
suttas.blurb: While staying overnight in a potter’s workshop, the Buddha has a chance encounter with a monk who does not recognize him. They have a long and profound discussion based on the four elements. This is one of the most insightful and moving discourses in the canon.

Overinterpreting Sāriputta’s reply, Kaḷāra reports to the Buddha that Sāriputta had claimed to have ended all defilements. The Buddha calls Sāriputta to clarify his statements, and Sāriputta explains in terms of dependent origination.

suttas.blurb: The Buddha explains diverse methods for overcoming diverse kinds of defilements.

suttas.title: Unblemished
suttas.blurb: The Buddha’s chief disciples, Sāriputta and Moggallāna, use a simile of a tarnished bowl to illustrate the blemishes of the mind and conduct. They emphasize how the crucial thing is not so much whether there are blemishes, but whether we are aware of them.

suttas.title: All the Defilements
suttas.blurb: The diverse problems of the spiritual journey demand a diverse range of responses. Rather than applying the same solution to every problem, the Buddha outlines seven methods of dealing with defilements, each of which works in certain cases.

name: Perfected Ones & Perfected (Ones)
description: 10 suttas on perfected ones, perfected, ones, ones perfected (SN dominant)

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin called “Harmless” visits the Buddha, who teaches him the true meaning of harmlessness.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: Hearing that a brahmin had gone forth under the Buddha, another brahmin was upset. But once again the Buddha calms him down, pointing out that anger only harms oneself.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: The brahmin Suddhika makes the strikingly Buddhist assertion that mere austerities don’t lead to purity; only knowledge and conduct do.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin with matted hair asks the Buddha how we can become disentangled. This short set of verses became one of the most important in all of Theravāda Buddhism when it was used as the cornerstone of the commentarial treatise Visuddhimagga (Path of Purification).

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin visits the Buddha and abuses him, but the Buddha responds only with patience.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin conducting a fire sacrifice invites the Buddha to accept the blessed milk-rice, claiming that the Buddha is a true brahmin.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin visits the Buddha and unleashes a tirade of abuse, but the Buddha refuses to accept it, showing that he remains free of anger.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: When a brahmin lady expresses her confidence in the Triple Gem, her husband is annoyed. She encourages him to visit the Buddha, who soothes his anger.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin is searching for his lost oxen when he sees the Buddha meditating peacefully in the forest. He laments the many sorrows of his life, celebrating the Buddha’s happiness and freedom from such sorrows.

suttas.title: 1. The Perfected Ones
suttas.blurb: A brahmin conducting a fire sacrifice wishes to offer the sacrificial cake. He sees the Buddha meditating and wants to give it to him, but hesitates when he realizes he is a shaven-headed ascetic. He asks the Buddha’s caste, but the Buddha argues that conduct is the important thing, not caste. The Buddha gives a metaphorical reinterpretation of the elements of brahmanical ritual.
